topics.title: Familiarization with 8051 Based Kit Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML407 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization with 8051 Based Kit Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Familiarization with 8051 Based Kit Programming** is the foundational laboratory exercise in the *PCBML407 - Microcontrollers and Interfacing Lab* course wherein students gain hands-on exposure to the **Intel 8051 family of 8-bit microcontrollers**, the **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** used to author firmware (typically **Keil µVision IDE**), and the **target hardware evaluation kit** (such as the **NXP/Philips P89V51RD2**, **Atmel AT89C51/S51**, or generic **8051 trainer kits**) used to physically execute the developed machine code.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this experiment is mapped to **Course Outcome CO1** and the **Practical Skill Outcome PSO1**, emphasizing the development of competencies in:

- Identifying the architectural blocks of the 8051 microcontroller (ALU, Registers, ROM, RAM, I/O Ports, Timers, Serial Port, Interrupt Controller).
- Authoring basic **assembly language programs** using the 8051 instruction set (MOV, ADD, SUB, INC, DJNZ, ACALL, LCALL, SJMP, etc.).
- Cross-compiling, assembling, and generating the **HEX file** through Keil µVision.
- Downloading (burning) the HEX file into the on-chip **Flash ROM** of the 8051 device using a programmer (e.g., **Flash Magic**, **ISP Programmer**, or Universal USB Programmer).
- Executing the program on the actual hardware kit and verifying the output using LEDs, switches, or the **LCD module** integrated onto the trainer board.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment carries **continuous evaluation weightage** under the internal practical assessment scheme. Students are expected to record the **aim, apparatus required, procedure, algorithm, assembly program, observations, result, and viva answers** in their lab record as per KTU's prescribed format.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you have just received a brand-new **digital calculator** but with no buttons or display. Inside the box is a powerful **CPU chip** that can do math, but it is dormant — it has no instructions.

> **Analogy — The Recipe Book Inside the Chip:**
>
> Think of the 8051 microcontroller chip as a **master chef** who knows every cooking technique in the world but is currently **standing idle in an empty kitchen**. He has:
>
> - **Hands (I/O Ports)** — ready to chop, stir, or press,
> - **A notepad (RAM)** — to remember temporary recipes,
> - **A permanent recipe book (ROM/Flash)** — to store long-term recipes,
> - **A clock on the wall (Oscillator)** — ticking at a steady rhythm,
> - **A timer alarm (Timers/Counters)** — to track cooking durations.
>
> **Programming the 8051 kit** is the act of **writing a recipe (assembly program), translating it into the chef's exact language (machine code via assembler), pasting it into the chef's permanent notebook (burning HEX into Flash ROM), and then telling the chef: "Now read the recipe and start cooking!"**
>
> When you press the **RUN/RESET button** on the trainer kit, the chef (CPU) begins executing the recipe (program) you stored, using his hands (ports) to manipulate external ingredients (LEDs, motors, LCDs) connected to his kitchen counter (breadboard area of the kit).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology (Bolded Constants)

The following engineering constants and terminology are integral to the experiment:

| Term | Standard Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **8051 Data Bus Width** | **8 bits** |
| **8051 Address Bus Width** | **16 bits** (can address up to **64 KB** of external memory) |
| **On-chip ROM (Program Memory)** | **4 KB** (standard 8051) |
| **On-chip RAM (Data Memory)** | **128 bytes** (standard 8051) |
| **On-chip RAM (Expanded 8052)** | **256 bytes** |
| **Number of I/O Ports** | **4 ports × 8 pins = 32 I/O lines** (P0, P1, P2, P3) |
| **Timer/Counters** | **2 × 16-bit** (Timer 0, Timer 1) |
| **Serial Port** | **1 full-duplex UART** |
| **Interrupt Sources** | **5 (or 6 in 8052)** |
| **Clock Frequency (typical kit)** | **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz** crystal |
| **Machine Cycle** | **12 oscillator periods** (so **1 µs per machine cycle at 12 MHz**) |
| **Instruction Cycle Range** | **1 to 4 machine cycles** |
| **Assembler Used (KTU Lab Standard)** | **Keil µVision IDE (C51 Compiler/Assembler)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** This exact crystal frequency is preferred because it produces clean, error-free baud rate divisions for the UART (e.g., 9600 baud). At **12 MHz**, the baud rate divisor produces a fractional value, leading to serial communication errors.

---

## 1.4 Learning Outcomes of This Experiment

Upon successful completion, the KTU 2024 Scheme expects the student to:

1. **Identify** the major hardware components on the 8051 trainer kit (CPU socket, crystal oscillator, reset circuit, power supply, I/O ports, LEDs, 7-segment display, LCD, key matrix, ADC, etc.).
2. **Write**, **assemble**, and **simulate** simple 8051 assembly language programs.
3. **Download** the generated HEX file into the on-chip Flash using a programmer tool.
4. **Execute** the program and **verify** output on LEDs or the LCD.
5. **Debug** basic assembly errors (syntax errors, logical errors).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Architectural Block Model — Decomposition

The 8051 microcontroller, originally introduced by **Intel Corporation in 1980**, is the canonical **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) 8-bit Harvard architecture** microcontroller. Every KTU lab experiment begins by understanding these internal blocks:

### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU) Block
- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs 8-bit arithmetic (`ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`) and logical (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`) operations.
- **Accumulator (A) Register:** The single most important 8-bit register. Almost every ALU operation involves the Accumulator as one of the operands.
- **B Register:** Used primarily by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions. For non-arithmetic instructions, it can serve as a general-purpose scratch register.
- **Program Counter (PC):** A 16-bit register that always points to the next instruction to be executed in program memory. After reset, **PC = 0000H**.
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** An 8-bit register pointing to the top of the stack in internal RAM. After reset, **SP = 07H**, and the stack grows **upwards** (towards higher addresses).
- **Program Status Word (PSW):** An 8-bit register containing the **flag bits** that reflect the result of the last ALU operation.
  - Bit 7 → **CY** (Carry Flag)
  - Bit 6 → **AC** (Auxiliary Carry — for BCD arithmetic)
  - Bit 5 → **F0** (User-defined Flag 0)
  - Bit 4 → **RS1** (Register Bank Select 1)
  - Bit 3 → **RS0** (Register Bank Select 0)
  - Bit 2 → **OV** (Overflow Flag)
  - Bit 1 → **—** (Reserved)
  - Bit 0 → **P** (Parity Flag — even parity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Register Bank Selection Rule:** `RS1 RS0 = 00` selects **Bank 0** (addresses 00H–07H), `01` selects **Bank 1** (08H–0FH), `10` selects **Bank 2** (10H–17H), `11` selects **Bank 3** (18H–1FH). This is a high-yield KTU viva question.

### 2.1.2 Memory Organization Block

The 8051 follows the **Harvard architecture**, meaning **Program Memory** and **Data Memory** are physically separate address spaces.

| Memory Type | Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal RAM (Data Memory)** | 00H – 7FH | 128 bytes | General-purpose RAM, Register Banks, Bit-addressable area, SFRs occupy 80H–FFH in the unified address space for chips like 8052. |
| **Bit-Addressable Area** | 20H – 2FH | 16 bytes | 128 individually addressable bits (bit 00H to bit 7FH). |
| **Register Banks (R0–R7)** | 00H – 1FH | 4 banks × 8 bytes | Quick access registers via `R0`–`R7` mnemonics. |
| **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** | 80H – FFH | 128 bytes (many unused) | Control registers like P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP, ACC, B, SP, PSW, etc. |
| **Internal ROM (Program Memory)** | 0000H – 0FFFH | 4 KB | Stores user program code. |
| **External Program Memory** | 0000H – FFFFH | up to 64 KB | Accessed via PSEN̅ signal (if EA̅ = 0). |
| **External Data Memory** | 0000H – FFFFH | up to 64 KB | Accessed via MOVX instruction (uses RD̅/WR̅ signals). |

### 2.1.3 I/O Port Structure

The 8051 has **four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports** named **P0, P1, P2, P3**.

- **Port 0 (P0.0–P0.7):** Open-drain. Used as the **multiplexed lower byte address/data bus** when external memory is interfaced. Requires external pull-up resistors.
- **Port 1 (P1.0–P1.7):** Quasi-bidirectional (with internal pull-ups). Used as a general-purpose I/O port.
- **Port 2 (P2.0–P2.7):** Quasi-bidirectional. Used as the **upper byte address bus** when external memory is interfaced.
- **Port 3 (P3.0–P3.7):** Quasi-bidirectional. Each pin has an **alternate function**:
  - `P3.0 → RxD` (Serial Input)
  - `P3.1 → TxD` (Serial Output)
  - `P3.2 → INT0̅` (External Interrupt 0)
  - `P3.3 → INT1̅` (External Interrupt 1)
  - `P3.4 → T0` (Timer 0 Input)
  - `P3.5 → T1` (Timer 1 Input)
  - `P3.6 → WR̅` (External Data Memory Write Strobe)
  - `P3.7 → RD̅` (External Data Memory Read Strobe)

> [!NOTE]
> **SFRs P0, P1, P2, P3** are located at SFR addresses **80H, 90H, A0H, B0H** respectively. Writing to these SFRs writes the latch that drives the pins.

### 2.1.4 Oscillator and Machine Cycle Logic

The 8051 derives its timing from an external quartz crystal connected between pins **XTAL1 (Pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (Pin 18)**.

$$\text{One Machine Cycle} = \frac{12}{\text{Oscillator Frequency}}$$

For a **12 MHz crystal**:

$$\text{Machine Cycle Period} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1\ \mu\text{s}$$

For an **11.0592 MHz crystal**:

$$\text{Machine Cycle Period} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in the lab?** When you write a delay loop using `DJNZ`, you must calculate the exact delay in seconds based on the crystal frequency installed on your kit. KTU lab viva frequently asks: *"If your crystal is 12 MHz, how many machine cycles does your loop consume?"*

---

## 2.2 The 8051 Programming Model — Why and How

### 2.2.1 Why Assembly Programming?
Although modern firmware is written in **Embedded C**, the KTU 2024 Scheme mandates initial exposure to **assembly language** because:

- It reveals the **exact hardware behavior** of the chip.
- It teaches **memory-mapped I/O** and **register-level thinking**.
- It is **deterministic** — the execution time of every instruction is known precisely, which is critical for real-time systems.

### 2.2.2 The How — The Assembly-to-Execution Pipeline

The student's workflow for the familiarization experiment follows this 5-stage pipeline:

$$\text{Stage 1: Write Source Code (.ASM)} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2: Assemble using A51/Keil} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3: Link to produce HEX file} \rightarrow \text{Stage 4: Download HEX to Flash via ISP} \rightarrow \text{Stage 5: Press RESET to execute}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| Delay Loop (single-level DJNZ) | $T_{delay} = N_{iter} \times T_{MC} \times 2$ | $N_{iter}$ = DJNZ count, factor 2 = `DJNZ` + `DJNZ` overhead (typical nested form) |
| Nested Delay (two-level DJNZ) | $T_{delay} = (R0 \times R1 + R0) \times T_{MC}$ | seconds, for `DJNZ R1, INNER; DJNZ R0, OUTER` |
| Baud Rate for UART Mode 1 | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{32 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bits per second |
| TH1 value for 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{11059200}{32 \times 9600} = 253 = \text{FDH}$ | dimensionless integer |
| Parallel Address Space Limit | $2^{16} = 65536 = 64\ \text{KB}$ | bytes |
| Internal RAM (data) Limit | $2^7 = 128$ bytes (8051) | bytes |
| Register Bank Address (Bank N) | $\text{Start} = 8N$, $\text{End} = 8N + 7$ | hex address |
| SFR P0 Address | 80H | hex |
| SFR P1 Address | 90H | hex |
| SFR P2 Address | A0H | hex |
| SFR P3 Address | B0H | hex |
| SFR Accumulator (A) Address | E0H (bit-addressable) | hex |
| SFR B Register Address | F0H | hex |
| SFR SP Default After Reset | 07H | hex |
| SFR PC Default After Reset | 0000H | hex |
| SFR PSW Default After Reset | 00H | hex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Note for KTU Examiners:** In all rendered equations above, the conditional modulus and divide operations are written using explicit fraction notation to preserve markdown table integrity. In handwritten exams, the same expressions appear as $|x|$ or vertical-bar notation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051, despite being decades old, is **still manufactured in massive volumes** and remains a critical workhorse in:

| Industry Sector | Real-World 8051 Application Example |
|---|---|
| **Home Appliances** | Washing machine controllers, microwave oven control boards, air-conditioner remote receivers |
| **Automotive (Legacy ECUs)** | Dashboard instrument clusters, basic body control modules (BCM), wiper controllers |
| **Toys & Consumer Electronics** | Remote-controlled toys, electronic greeting cards, low-end music players |
| **Industrial Control** | Stepper motor drivers, conveyor belt sequencing controllers, temperature data loggers |
| **Medical Devices (Low-End)** | Digital blood pressure monitors, glucose meters, infusion pumps |
| **Educational Sector** | Engineering lab kits (which is exactly your KTU lab!) |

The reason for its enduring relevance is its **simplicity**, **ultra-low cost** (often below $0.50 per unit), **extensive documentation**, and the existence of a **massive ecosystem** of compilers, programmers, and reference designs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Classical "Blink LED" Demonstration — Exhaustive Walkthrough

In the KTU familiarization experiment, the **canonical first program** is the **LED blinking program**. The student connects an LED (via a current-limiting resistor of **220 Ω** or **330 Ω**) to a Port pin (typically **P1.0**), writes an assembly program to alternate the pin high and low with a visible delay, and verifies the blink on the actual kit.

### 3.1.1 The Hardware Setup

```
       +5V
        |
       [220 Ω]
        |
        +-------- P1.0 of 8051
        |
       ---  (LED, anode at top)
        |
       GND
```

### 3.1.2 The Complete Assembly Source Code (`.ASM` file)

```asm
; ================================================
; PROGRAM: LED Blink Demonstration for 8051 Kit
; COURSE : PCBML407 - KTU 2024 Scheme
; AUTHOR : [Student Name / Roll No]
; DATE   : [Date of Experiment]
; CRYSTAL: 11.0592 MHz (verify on your kit)
; ================================================

        ORG     0000H           ; Origin directive: Program starts at 0000H
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Long Jump to MAIN (skip interrupt vector space)

        ORG     0030H           ; Place MAIN at address 0030H (beyond interrupt vectors)

MAIN:   ACALL   DELAY           ; Initial delay to allow power stabilization

LOOP:   SETB    P1.0            ; Set P1.0 HIGH  -> LED turns ON
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Call delay subroutine (LED remains ON)
        CLR     P1.0            ; Clear P1.0 LOW -> LED turns OFF
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Call delay subroutine (LED remains OFF)
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Short Jump back to LOOP (infinite blinking)

; ================================================
; DELAY SUBROUTINE (Approx 250 ms @ 11.0592 MHz)
; ================================================
DELAY:  MOV     R0, #0FFH       ; Load outer counter = 255
OUTER:  MOV     R1, #0FFH       ; Load inner counter = 255
INNER:  DJNZ    R1, INNER       ; Decrement R1, loop if not zero (255 iterations)
        DJNZ    R0, OUTER       ; Decrement R0, loop if not zero (255 iterations)
        RET                     ; Return to caller

        END                     ; End of source file
```

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Execution Trace — First 8 Cycles

Let us trace what happens from the moment power is applied to the kit:

| Step | PC (Hex) | Instruction | Action | Result on Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000 | `LJMP MAIN` | PC loaded with address `0030H` (long jump to MAIN) | Program Counter jumps over ISR vectors. |
| 2 | 0030 | `ACALL DELAY` | Pushes PC+2 (0033H) onto stack at address 08H; SP becomes 09H. Jumps to DELAY at 0033H. | — |
| 3 | 0033 | `MOV R0, #0FFH` | Loads R0 with 255 decimal = FFH. R0 is in Register Bank 0 (address 00H–07H). Uses 2 bytes of ROM, 1 machine cycle. | — |
| 4 | 0035 | `MOV R1, #0FFH` | Loads R1 with 255. Uses 1 machine cycle. | — |
| 5 | 0037 | `DJNZ R1, INNER` | R1 decremented from 255 to 254. Since R1 ≠ 0, jump back to INNER (relative offset = -2 → PC becomes 0037H). | — |
| 6 | (loop) | Repeat step 5 | R1 decrements until R1 = 0. Total iterations of INNER = 255. | — |
| 7 | 0039 | `DJNZ R0, OUTER` | R0 decrements from 255 to 254. R0 ≠ 0, so jump back to OUTER. R1 reloaded with 0FFH. | — |
| 8 | (loop) | Repeat steps 5–7 | Inner loop runs 255 times for each outer iteration. Total inner iterations = 255 × 255 = 65,025. | — |

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Exact Delay Time

The total number of machine cycles consumed by the nested DELAY loop must be calculated to determine the ON-time and OFF-time of the LED.

**Inner loop (`DJNZ R1, INNER`) — One Iteration Cycle:**
- `DJNZ R1, INNER` itself → **2 machine cycles**
- Auto-jump (taken) → **2 machine cycles**
- Total per inner iteration → **4 machine cycles**

**Outer loop body (`MOV R1, #FFH` + inner DJNZ loop):**
- `MOV R1, #0FFH` → **1 machine cycle**
- Inner DJNZ loop (255 iterations) → **255 × 4 = 1020 machine cycles**
- `DJNZ R0, OUTER` → **2 machine cycles**
- Auto-jump (taken) → **2 machine cycles**
- Total per outer iteration → **1 + 1020 + 2 + 2 = 1025 machine cycles**

**Total DELAY subroutine machine cycles:**
$$N_{total} = 255 \times 1025 + 2 \quad \text{(final R0=0 fall-through)} = 261375 + 2 = 261377 \text{ cycles}$$

**Converting to time (assuming 11.0592 MHz crystal):**

$$T_{delay} = \frac{N_{total} \times 12}{f_{osc}}$$

$$T_{delay} = \frac{261377 \times 12}{11059200}$$

$$T_{delay} = \frac{3136524}{11059200} \approx 0.2836\ \text{seconds}$$

So the LED is ON for ~**284 ms** and OFF for ~**284 ms**, giving a total blink period of ~**568 ms** (blink frequency ~**1.76 Hz**), which is **clearly visible to the human eye**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Exam Tip:** If your crystal is **12 MHz** instead of 11.0592 MHz, the same code produces a delay of approximately **261 ms**. **Always verify the crystal frequency stamped on your kit before recording the result in your lab record.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Lab Programs

The familiarization experiment typically includes several other standard programs. The analytical matrix below summarizes them:

| Program # | Program Title | Key Instructions Used | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED ON / OFF | `SETB P1.0`, `CLR P1.0`, `SJMP LOOP` | LED glows continuously or stays off. |
| 2 | LED Blinking (single LED) | `SETB`, `CLR`, `ACALL DELAY`, `SJMP` | LED blinks at ~1.76 Hz. |
| 3 | Multiple LED Patterns | `MOV A, #DATA`, `MOV P1, A`, `RL A`, `RR A`, `SJMP` | Running light / chasing pattern. |
| 4 | LED Toggling Using XOR | `XRL P1, #01H`, `ACALL DELAY` | Single LED toggles using single instruction. |
| 5 | Data Transfer to RAM | `MOV 30H, #0AAH`, `MOV 31H, #55H` | Memory locations 30H and 31H contain AA and 55 respectively (verifiable using Keil Memory Window). |
| 6 | Arithmetic — Sum of Two Bytes | `MOV A, #25H`, `ADD A, #34H`, `MOV 30H, A` | Sum 59H stored at RAM[30H], Carry flag reflects overflow. |
| 7 | Logical Operations | `MOV A, #0F0H`, `ANL A, #0FH`, `MOV P1, A` | Result 00H written to P1 (all LEDs OFF). |
| 8 | I/O Port Read-and-Write | `MOV P1, #0FFH` (configure as input), `MOV A, P1`, `CPL A`, `MOV P1, A` | LEDs show the **complement** of the switch positions. |

---

## 3.3 The Keil µVision IDE Workflow — Step-by-Step Derivation

The student must demonstrate competence in the following **10-step workflow** when using Keil µVision IDE (the KTU-prescribed tool):

| Step | Action | Output / Screenshot |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Launch **Keil µVision** (typically Version 4 or 5). | Splash screen appears. |
| **2** | Click **Project → New µVision Project**. Save with a meaningful name (e.g., `led_blink`). | Project file `.uvproj` created. |
| **3** | In the **Device Database** dialog, select the manufacturer (e.g., *Atmel*) and device (e.g., *AT89C51* or *P89V51RD2*). | CPU chip selected. |
| **4** | Right-click **Source Group 1 → Add New Item → C File or Asm File** (`led_blink.asm`). | Editor window opens. |
| **5** | Type the assembly source code in the editor. Save with **Ctrl+S**. | `.A51` or `.ASM` file saved. |
| **6** | Click the **Options for Target** button (magic wand icon). In the *Output* tab, check **Create HEX File**. | Project configured to emit HEX. |
| **7** | Click **Build (F7)** or **Rebuild All**. The **Build Output** window shows `Program Size: data=9.0 xdata=0 code=30`. | `.HEX` file generated in project folder. |
| **8** | Open the **Debug** menu → **Start/Stop Debug Session (Ctrl+F5)**. Use **Step (F11)** or **Run (F5)** to execute. | Simulator window opens showing registers, memory, ports. |
| **9** | To program the physical kit, exit debug mode and use **Flash Magic** (for NXP P89V51RD2) or **ProgISP** (for AT89C51) to load the HEX file via the **USB-to-ISP** cable. | HEX file transferred to on-chip Flash. |
| **10** | Press the **RESET** button on the trainer kit. The program begins execution immediately. | LED blinks visibly on the kit. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Error Resolution Matrix:**
>
> | Error Message in Keil | Cause | Fix |
> |---|---|---|
> | `Error: A51: Syntax Error` | Typo in mnemonic (e.g., `MOV` written as `M0V`). | Retype carefully; mnemonics are case-insensitive but spelling-sensitive. |
> | `Error: Undefined Symbol` | A label is referenced but never defined. | Check for typos in label names. |
> | `Warning: Level 2: Uncalled Segment` | A subroutine was defined but never invoked via `ACALL`/`LCALL`. | Add the call or remove the dead code. |
> | `Target not found` (in Flash Magic) | Wrong COM port selected, or baud rate mismatch. | Verify USB-Serial COM port in Device Manager; set baud to 9600. |
> | LED does not blink after RESET | Crystal not oscillating, or HEX file not properly flashed. | Verify crystal with oscilloscope; re-flash and confirm. |

---

## 3.4 Demonstration of Bit Manipulation (Sub-Exercise)

Many KTU viva questions probe the student's grasp of **bit-level operations**. A typical demonstration:

> **Task:** Toggle P1.7 using only the XOR instruction, without using a separate delay routine.

```asm
        ORG     0000H
START:  MOV     A, P1          ; Read current P1 value into A
        XRL     A, #80H        ; XOR with 10000000b — toggles ONLY bit 7
        MOV     P1, A          ; Write result back to P1
        SJMP    START          ; Infinite loop (toggles at instruction rate)
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation (first 3 iterations):

| Iteration | Initial P1 (Binary) | `MOV A, P1` (A value) | A XOR 80H | Final P1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000 0000 | 00H | 80H | 1000 0000 (LED ON) |
| 2 | 1000 0000 | 80H | 00H | 0000 0000 (LED OFF) |
| 3 | 0000 0000 | 00H | 80H | 1000 0000 (LED ON) |

> The LED toggles every ~**2 µs** (since each instruction = 1–2 machine cycles at 12 MHz), which is **far too fast for the human eye to perceive** — it will appear to be glowing at half intensity. To see a clear blink, **a delay routine is mandatory**.

---

## 3.5 Practical Output Verification — Reading Switches and Driving LEDs

A common extension of the familiarization experiment involves **interfacing DIP switches** as inputs and **LEDs** as outputs:

```asm
; READ P1.0..P1.3 (switches), DISPLAY INVERTED VALUE ON P1.4..P1.7 (LEDs)
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     P1, #0FH       ; Configure lower nibble of P1 as INPUT (write 1s), upper as output
LOOP:   MOV     A, P1          ; Read entire P1
        SWAP    A              ; Swap upper and lower nibbles
        CPL     A              ; Complement to invert
        ANL     A, #0F0H       ; Mask to keep only upper nibble
        MOV     P1, A          ; Write back to P1 (switches unaffected)
        SJMP    LOOP
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation:

| Switch State (P1.0–P1.3) | A after `MOV A,P1` | A after `SWAP A` | A after `CPL A` | A after `ANL A,#F0H` | Final P1.4–P1.7 LED State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0000 | 0FH | F0H | 0FH | 00H | OFF OFF OFF OFF |
| 0101 | 5FH | F5H | 0AH | 00H | OFF OFF OFF OFF |
| 1111 | FFH | FFH | 00H | 00H | OFF OFF OFF OFF |
| 1010 | AFH | FAH | 05H | 00H | OFF OFF OFF OFF |

> [!WARNING]
> **A subtle hardware trap:** Because P1 is read-modified-written (the same port is used for both input and output), the input switches will appear "stuck" if the LEDs on the output pins are not isolated properly. Many trainer kits provide **buffered LED banks** precisely to avoid this issue.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

The following Mermaid diagrams comply with the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Node Identifier Alpha Rule**: every node ID begins with an alphabetic prefix (e.g., `kitBlock1`, `cpuCore`, `regBank0`) and no reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as standalone node names.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — The 8051 Trainer Kit

This diagram maps the **interaction flow** between the major functional blocks of a typical 8051-based trainer kit (such as the **NXP P89V51RD2 Development Board** or the **AT89C51 Universal Trainer Kit**).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph powerSupply["POWER SUPPLY UNIT 5V / 12V DC"]
        psInput["AC Adapter 9V DC"]
        psReg["LM7805 Voltage Regulator"]
        psOutput["5V DC Rail to All Blocks"]
        psInput --> psReg --> psOutput
    end

    subgraph cpuCore["8051 CPU SOCKET BLOCK"]
        crystal["Crystal Oscillator 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz"]
        cpuSocket["40-pin DIP Socket for 8051 IC"]
        resetCircuit["RESET Circuit Manual Push Button plus 10uF Capacitor plus 8.2k Resistor"]
        cpuSocket --- crystal
        cpuSocket --- resetCircuit
    end

    subgraph memorySection["MEMORY EXPANSION BLOCK"]
        rom["4 KB Internal Flash ROM inside 8051"]
        ram["128 Byte Internal RAM inside 8051"]
        xram["External 32 KB SRAM 62256 optional"]
        rom --- ram
        ram --- xram
    end

    subgraph ioSection["INPUT OUTPUT PERIPHERALS BLOCK"]
        leds["8 LED Bank connected to P1 with 220 ohm resistors"]
        switches["8 DIP Switches connected to P1 with 10k pullups"]
        lcdModule["16x2 LCD Module connected to P2 and P3"]
        keypad["4x4 Matrix Keypad connected to P1 and P3"]
        sevenSeg["Common Cathode 7-Segment Display connected to P0"]
        leds --- switches
        lcdModule --- keypad
        sevenSeg --- leds
    end

    subgraph progSection["PROGRAMMING INTERFACE BLOCK"]
        ispConnector["ISP Header 10-pin Standard"]
        usbSerial["USB to Serial CH340 Chip"]
        flashMagic["Host PC Tool Flash Magic or ProgISP"]
        flashMagic --> usbSerial --> ispConnector
    end

    psOutput -.powers.-> cpuCore
    psOutput -.powers.-> memorySection
    psOutput -.powers.-> ioSection
    psOutput -.powers.-> progSection
    cpuCore -- "Address and Data Bus" --> memorySection
    cpuCore -- "P0 P1 P2 P3 Ports" --> ioSection
    progSection -- "Loads HEX to Flash ROM" --> memorySection
```

---

## 4.3 Programming Workflow Flowchart — From Source Code to Hardware Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Stage 1 Student writes .ASM source code in Keil Editor"]
    stepB["Stage 2 Keil A51 Assembler translates mnemonics to machine code"]
    stepC["Stage 3 Linker produces absolute object file"]
    stepD["Stage 4 OH51 Object to HEX converter creates .HEX file"]
    stepE["Stage 5 Flash Magic or ProgISP tool on Host PC"]
    stepF["Stage 6 HEX transmitted via USB to Serial Cable to ISP Header on Kit"]
    stepG["Stage 7 Bootloader inside 8051 writes HEX bytes into internal Flash ROM"]
    stepH["Stage 8 Student presses RESET button on Kit"]
    stepI["Stage 9 CPU fetches first instruction from address 0000H and begins execution"]
    stepJ["Stage 10 LED lights up or LCD displays message PROOF of correct program"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ
```

## 4.4 8051 Internal CPU Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph aluBlock["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT"]
        aluCore["8-bit ALU"]
        accReg["Accumulator A 8-bit at SFR E0H"]
        bReg["B Register 8-bit at SFR F0H"]
        tmpReg["TMP1 TMP2 internal scratch registers"]
        aluCore --- accReg
        aluCore --- bReg
        aluCore --- tmpReg
    end

    subgraph controlBlock["CONTROL UNIT"]
        pcReg["Program Counter 16-bit"]
        dpTR["DPTR 16-bit Data Pointer SFR 82H 83H"]
        instrReg["Instruction Register"]
        decoder["Instruction Decoder PLA"]
        timingBlock["Timing and Control 12 clock divider"]
        pcReg --> instrReg --> decoder --> timingBlock
        dpTR --- pcReg
    end

    subgraph sfrBlock["SPECIAL FUNCTION REGISTERS SFR SPACE 80H to FFH"]
        portSfr["P0 P1 P2 P3 Port Latches"]
        timerSfr["TCON TMOD TH0 TL0 TH1 TL1"]
        serialSfr["SCON SBUF"]
        interruptSfr["IE IP"]
        pwrSfr["PCON"]
        portSfr --- timerSfr
        serialSfr --- interruptSfr
        pwrSfr --- portSfr
    end

    subgraph memoryBus["MEMORY INTERFACE"]
        progBus["16-bit Program Address Bus to ROM"]
        dataBus["8-bit Internal Data Bus to RAM and SFRs"]
        extBus["16-bit External Address Bus for MOVX"]
        progBus --- dataBus
        dataBus --- extBus
    end

    controlBlock -- "Control signals to ALU" --> aluBlock
    aluBlock -- "Operation results" --> sfrBlock
    controlBlock -- "Address lines" --> memoryBus
    sfrBlock -- "Bidirectional data" --> memoryBus
```

## 4.5 Register Bank Selection Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rsBit["PSW Register bits RS1 RS0"]
    decision1{"RS1 RS0 equal 00?"}
    decision2{"RS1 RS0 equal 01?"}
    decision3{"RS1 RS0 equal 10?"}
    decision4{"RS1 RS0 equal 11?"}
    bank0["BANK 0 R0 to R7 mapped to RAM 00H to 07H"]
    bank1["BANK 1 R0 to R7 mapped to RAM 08H to 0FH"]
    bank2["BANK 2 R0 to R7 mapped to RAM 10H to 17H"]
    bank3["BANK 3 R0 to R7 mapped to RAM 18H to 1FH"]

    rsBit --> decision1
    decision1 -- YES --> bank0
    decision1 -- NO --> decision2
    decision2 -- YES --> bank1
    decision2 -- NO --> decision3
    decision3 -- YES --> bank2
    decision3 -- NO --> decision4
    decision4 -- YES --> bank3
```

## 4.6 Instruction Execution Cycle Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fetch1["Fetch Opcode from Program Memory at PC"]
    decode1["Decode Opcode in Instruction Decoder"]
    exec1["Execute in ALU or move data on internal bus"]
    store1["Write Result to Destination Register or SFR or Memory"]
    next1["Increment PC by 1 2 or 3 bytes depending on instruction"]
    loop1["Repeat infinitely until RESET or Power Off"]

    fetch1 --> decode1 --> exec1 --> store1 --> next1 --> loop1
    loop1 -.-> fetch1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Pattern Alignment Note

All questions below are modeled on **actual KTU University lab examinations** and the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) viva-voce pattern** for the 2024 Scheme. The question distribution strictly follows:

- **Part A (Short Answer, 3 marks each):** Conceptual definitions, register identification, instruction explanation.
- **Part B (Long Answer, 14 marks each):** Full program write-up, execution trace, hardware interface explanation.

---

## 5.2 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define the term "Register Bank" in the 8051 microcontroller. How many register banks are available and how are they selected?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 8051 microcontroller provides **four register banks**, each consisting of **eight 8-bit registers** named `R0` through `R7`. These banks occupy the lower **32 bytes of internal RAM** as follows:

| Bank | RAM Address Range | Selection Bits (RS1, RS0) |
|---|---|---|
| Bank 0 | 00H – 07H | 00 |
| Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH | 01 |
| Bank 2 | 10H – 17H | 10 |
| Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH | 11 |

The bank is selected by the **RS1 and RS0 bits of the PSW register**. After reset, **Bank 0 is selected** (RS1 = 0, RS0 = 0). Only one bank is active at a time, allowing the programmer to switch contexts quickly during interrupt handling.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the difference between the `SJMP`, `AJMP`, and `LJMP` jump instructions of the 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Instruction | Full Form | Jump Range | Opcode Size | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `SJMP rel_addr` | **Short Jump** | -128 to +127 bytes (relative to PC) | 2 bytes | Small forward/backward loops within ±127 bytes. |
| `AJMP addr11` | **Absolute Jump** | Within the current 2 KB page | 2 bytes | Mid-range jumps within the same 2 KB code page. |
| `LJMP addr16` | **Long Jump** | Anywhere in 64 KB program memory | 3 bytes | Jumps to any address in the entire program space (e.g., from `0000H` to `MAIN`). |

In the LED blink program, the **infinite loop uses `SJMP`** because the target label is just a few bytes backward. The **reset vector uses `LJMP`** because the `MAIN` routine may be placed at any arbitrary address (e.g., `0030H`).

---

## 5.3 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. Students may choose **either** Question A or Question B (internal choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of the 8051 microcontroller. List all Special Function Registers (SFRs) related to the I/O ports.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly language program to toggle all 8 LEDs connected to Port P1 with a 500 ms delay. Assume the crystal frequency is 11.0592 MHz. Show the delay calculation in detail.

#### Model Answer — Part (a):

The internal architecture of the 8051 microcontroller (refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.4**) consists of the following major units:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU):** Contains the **8-bit ALU**, the **Accumulator (A)**, the **B register**, and the **Program Status Word (PSW)**.

2. **Memory Organization:**
   - **4 KB of internal ROM** (program memory) at addresses `0000H`–`0FFFH`.
   - **128 bytes of internal RAM** (data memory) at addresses `00H`–`7FH`, of which the lower 32 bytes are the **four register banks** and the next 16 bytes (`20H`–`2FH`) are **bit-addressable**.
   - **External memory expansion** of up to **64 KB** for both program and data memory.

3. **I/O Ports:** Four 8-bit ports P0, P1, P2, P3 with their SFR addresses:

   | Port | SFR Address | Hex Address |
   |---|---|---|
   | P0 | 80H (bit-addressable) | 80H |
   | P1 | 90H (bit-addressable) | 90H |
   | P2 | A0H (bit-addressable) | A0H |
   | P3 | B0H (bit-addressable) | B0H |

4. **Timers/Counters:** Two 16-bit timers (Timer 0, Timer 1) controlled by `TMOD` and `TCON` SFRs.

5. **Serial Port:** Full-duplex UART controlled by `SCON` and `SBUF` SFRs.

6. **Interrupt Controller:** Five interrupt sources controlled by `IE` (Interrupt Enable) and `IP` (Interrupt Priority) SFRs.

#### Model Answer — Part (b):

**Target Delay:** 500 ms = 0.5 seconds

**Strategy:** Use a **three-level nested DJNZ loop** because a two-level loop with `R0 = R1 = 255` only produces ~284 ms at 11.0592 MHz.

**Calculation of Required Loop Counts:**

For a two-level nested loop, the total delay is:

$$T_{delay} = (R0 \times (R1 \times 4 + 2 + 2) + 2) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Setting $T_{delay} = 0.5$ s, $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz:

$$0.5 = (R0 \times (R1 \times 4 + 4) + 2) \times \frac{12}{11059200}$$

$$(R0 \times (R1 \times 4 + 4) + 2) = 0.5 \times \frac{11059200}{12} = 460800$$

So $R0 \times (R1 \times 4 + 4) = 460798$. Choosing $R0 = 250$, $R1 = 250$:

$$250 \times (250 \times 4 + 4) = 250 \times 1004 = 251000 \text{ machine cycles}$$

This is **less than** 460800. So we need a **third outer loop (R2)**:

$$R2 \times 251000 \approx 460800 \implies R2 \approx 1.84 \implies \text{take } R2 = 2$$

**Final Assembly Program:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     P1, #00H       ; Initially all LEDs OFF

LOOP:   MOV     A, #0FFH       ; A = 11111111b
        MOV     P1, A          ; All LEDs ON
        ACALL   DELAY          ; Wait 500 ms
        MOV     A, #00H
        MOV     P1, A          ; All LEDs OFF
        ACALL   DELAY          ; Wait 500 ms
        SJMP    LOOP

DELAY:  MOV     R2, #02H       ; Outer counter
L3:     MOV     R0, #0FAH      ; R0 = 250
L2:     MOV     R1, #0FAH      ; R1 = 250
L1:     DJNZ    R1, L1         ; Inner loop
        DJNZ    R0, L2         ; Middle loop
        DJNZ    R2, L3         ; Outer loop
        RET

        END
```

**Verification of delay:**

$$T_{delay} = (2 \times (250 \times (250 \times 4 + 4) + 2)) \times \frac{12}{11059200}$$

$$T_{delay} = (2 \times 251002) \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_{delay} \approx 0.5447 \text{ seconds}$$

Close to 500 ms — within KTU's accepted tolerance (±10%) for hardware demonstration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the function of the Program Counter (PC), Stack Pointer (SP), and Data Pointer (DPTR) registers in the 8051.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly program to read the lower nibble of P1 (connected to switches) and display its binary-coded decimal (BCD) equivalent on the upper nibble of P1 (connected to LEDs). Assume the input is always 0–9.

#### Model Answer — Part (a):

**1. Program Counter (PC):**
The PC is a **16-bit register** that always holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched from program memory. After reset, `PC = 0000H`, so the first instruction executed is the one at address `0000H`. The PC is automatically incremented by the number of bytes of the currently executing instruction (1, 2, or 3 bytes). Branch instructions like `SJMP`, `AJMP`, `LJMP`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `JNZ`, `JC`, etc. modify the PC explicitly. The PC can address up to **64 KB** of program memory ($2^{16} = 65536$).

**2. Stack Pointer (SP):**
The SP is an **8-bit register** that points to the top of the stack in internal RAM. After reset, `SP = 07H`, meaning the stack starts at RAM address `07H` and grows **upward** (towards higher addresses) as data is pushed. The instructions `PUSH` (increment SP, then write) and `POP` (read, then decrement SP) manipulate the stack. The `ACALL`, `LCALL`, and `RET` instructions use the stack implicitly to save and restore the return address. The user can reinitialize SP (e.g., `MOV SP, #60H`) to relocate the stack to higher RAM and free up the lower banks for register use.

**3. Data Pointer (DPTR):**
DPTR is a **16-bit register** composed of two 8-bit SFRs: the **high byte DPH (SFR 83H)** and the **low byte DPL (SFR 82H)**. It is used to access **external data memory** using the `MOVX A, @DPTR` and `MOVX @DPTR, A` instructions. It can also be used as a base address for **program memory look-up tables** via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. DPTR is the only 16-bit register in the 8051 that the programmer can directly manipulate.

#### Model Answer — Part (b):

**Hardware Setup:**
- Switches `SW1`–`SW4` connected to `P1.0`–`P1.3` (lower nibble as input).
- LEDs `LED1`–`LED4` connected to `P1.4`–`P1.7` (upper nibble as output).
- Switches pulled up with 10 kΩ; LEDs driven via 220 Ω resistors.

**Strategy:**
The input is a decimal digit (0–9). The BCD output is identical to the binary representation for digits 0–9. So we only need to **move the lower nibble to the upper nibble position**.

**Assembly Program:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     P1, #0FH       ; Configure lower nibble as input (write 1s),
                               ; upper nibble as output (any value initially)
LOOP:   MOV     A, P1          ; Read entire P1
        ANL     A, #0FH        ; Mask to keep only lower nibble (switches)
        SWAP    A              ; Move lower nibble to upper nibble position
        ANL     A, #0F0H       ; Clear any stray lower nibble bits
        MOV     P1, A          ; Write to upper nibble LEDs
        SJMP    LOOP

        END
```

**Execution Trace Example:**

| Switch Position (P1.3–P1.0) | Decimal | A after `MOV A,P1` | A after `ANL #0F` | A after `SWAP A` | A after `ANL #F0` | LED Output (P1.7–P1.4) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0000 | 0 | 0X | 00 | 00 | 00 | OFF OFF OFF OFF |
| 0101 | 5 | 5X | 05 | 50 | 50 | OFF ON OFF OFF |
| 1001 | 9 | 9X | 09 | 90 | 90 | ON OFF OFF OFF |
| 1111 | 15 | FX | 0F | F0 | F0 | ON ON ON ON |

> The program correctly displays the BCD value (which equals binary for 0–9) on the LEDs.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in the 8051 Familiarization Lab Exam:**
>
> 1. **Wrong crystal frequency assumption (–2 marks):** Students frequently assume 12 MHz when the actual kit has 11.0592 MHz, leading to incorrect delay calculations. **Always physically inspect the crystal can and verify with the lab technician.**
>
> 2. **Missing `ORG` directives (–2 marks):** Forgetting `ORG 0000H` at the start of the program, or not placing `MAIN` beyond the interrupt vector area (`0003H`, `000BH`, `0013H`, `001BH`, `0023H`), can cause the program to malfunction.
>
> 3. **Confusing `ACALL` and `LCALL` range (–1 mark):** `ACALL` can only jump within the current 2 KB page. If `DELAY` is placed beyond 2 KB from the call site, the assembler will produce an error.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to enable the HEX file output (–3 marks):** If the Keil project is not configured with **"Create HEX File"** in the Output tab, no HEX file is generated, and the student cannot program the kit. This is the **#1 reason** for "nothing happens after RESET" in the lab.
>
> 5. **Writing 0 to a port to make it an input (–2 marks):** Many students erroneously write `MOV P1, #00H` to configure P1 as input. In the 8051, **writing 1 makes the pin an input** (because the internal latch is set to high-impedance mode); writing 0 forces the output low.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the alternate functions of Port 3 (–1 mark):** Using P3.0 or P3.1 for general I/O without disabling the UART will cause interference.
>
> 7. **Not recording the observation table (–2 marks):** KTU mandates a tabulated observation for each program in the lab record. Omitting this is a direct mark loss.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] The **8051 is an 8-bit Harvard architecture CISC microcontroller** with **4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 32 I/O lines, 2 timers, 1 UART, and 5 interrupts**.
- [ ] The **four register banks** are at RAM addresses `00H–07H`, `08H–0FH`, `10H–17H`, `18H–1FH`, selected by **PSW.3 (RS0)** and **PSW.4 (RS1)**.
- [ ] **After reset:** `PC = 0000H`, `SP = 07H`, `ACC = 00H`, `B = 00H`, `PSW = 00H`, all ports = `FFH`.
- [ ] The **SFR addresses** of P0, P1, P2, P3 are **80H, 90H, A0H, B0H** — all bit-addressable.
- [ ] **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** At 12 MHz → 1 µs per machine cycle. At 11.0592 MHz → 1.085 µs per machine cycle.
- [ ] **Stack grows upward** (SP increments before each PUSH). Always reinitialize SP if you use Bank 1/2/3 as scratch registers.
- [ ] **Writing 1 to a port pin** makes it an **input** (high impedance). **Writing 0** drives it **low**. This is the opposite of some other microcontrollers.
- [ ] The **canonical LED blink program** uses `SETB`/`CLR` with `ACALL DELAY` inside an infinite `SJMP LOOP`.
- [ ] The **nested DJNZ delay** formula is $T_{delay} = (R_{outer} \times (R_{inner} \times 4 + 4) + 2) \times 12 / f_{osc}$.
- [ ] The **Keil µVision** workflow is: New Project → Select Device → Add `.ASM` file → Configure Output (Create HEX) → Build (F7) → Debug (Ctrl+F5).
- [ ] **Flash Magic** (for NXP P89V51RD2) or **ProgISP** (for Atmel AT89C51) are the standard tools to download HEX to the chip's Flash ROM.
- [ ] The **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred because it generates exact integer divisors for standard baud rates (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200).
- [ ] The **8051 instruction set** includes data transfer (`MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`), arithmetic (`ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL`, `DIV`), logical (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `CLR`, `RL`, `RR`, `RLC`, `RRC`), Boolean (`SETB`, `CLR bit`, `ANL bit`, `ORL bit`, `MOV bit`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`), and program branching (`SJMP`, `AJMP`, `LJMP`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI`, `NOP`).
- [ ] **Always record** in your lab record: (i) aim, (ii) apparatus, (iii) procedure/algorithm, (iv) full program with comments, (v) observation table, (vi) result with delay calculation, (vii) viva answers.
- [ ] The **KTU-expected viva questions** for this experiment include: "Why is the stack pointer initialized to 07H?", "What is the role of EA̅ pin?", "Difference between Harvard and Von Neumann architecture?", "What is the role of PSEN̅ signal?", "Explain the function of the ALE pin."

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Familiarization with 8051 Based Kit Programming**

topics.title: 8051 - Arithmetic and Logical Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBML407 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** 8051 - Arithmetic and Logical Operations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PCBML407 – Microcontrollers and Interfacing Lab*, the topic **"8051 – Arithmetic and Logical Operations"** is a foundational lab experiment under Module 2. Formally, it is defined as: > The study, analysis, and execution of the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** of the **Intel 8051 family of 8-bit microcontrollers** with specific focus on the subset of instructions that perform **arithmetic computations** (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, increment, decrement, and decimal adjust) and **logical/Boolean operations** (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotate, swap, and clear). The 8051 is an **8-bit** CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) microcontroller based on **Harvard architecture**, manufactured originally by Intel and now widely used in embedded education through the **AT89C51 / AT89S52** variants from Atmel/Microchip. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > Students are expected to **write, assemble, simulate, and execute** assembly language programs for arithmetic and logical operations using either **Keil µVision IDE**, **Proteus Simulation Suite**, or equivalent hardware kits (8051 trainer boards). The expected outcome is the ability to **interpret the Program Status Word (PSW) flags** after every operation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Pocket Calculator with a Brain Imagine a **very small, very fast digital calculator** that lives inside a chip on a circuit board. Unlike a normal calculator, this one does not just add and subtract — it can also: - **Multiply and divide** two numbers in a single instruction. - **Increment** a counter (like a digital odometer that ticks up by 1 every millisecond). - **Compare** two numbers and remember *which one is bigger* using tiny internal "sticky notes" called **flags**. - **Manipulate individual bits** of a number, like turning a single light switch ON or OFF without disturbing the others. The "sticky notes" inside the 8051 are the **bits of the Program Status Word (PSW) register** at SFR address **D0H** — Carry (CY), Auxiliary Carry (AC), Overflow (OV), and Parity (P). Every arithmetic/logical instruction automatically updates these notes so that **decision-making instructions** (like `JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, `DJNZ`) can react later. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions | Convention | Standard Value | |---|---| | Data bus width | **8 bits** | | Register width (A, B, R0–R7) | **8 bits** | | Address bus width | **16 bits** | | PSW (Program Status Word) address | **D0H** | | Accumulator (A) address | **E0H** | | B register address | **F0H** | | Maximum unsigned arithmetic range | **00H – FFH (0 to 255)** | | Two's complement signed range | **80H – 7FH (–128 to +127)** | | Clock machine cycle | **12 oscillator periods** | > [!NOTE] > The **two's complement signed range** of **–128 to +127** and the **OV flag** are the heart of signed arithmetic overflow detection in the 8051 — examiners love to test this! --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Arithmetic and Logical Instructions The 8051 instruction set divides arithmetic and logical operations into **four functional families**. Every instruction is examined below in structured bulleted form, with the *Why* and *How* explained. ### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Operations (Group 1: Addition Family) - **`ADD A, `** — Adds the source byte to the accumulator. - *Why:* The accumulator (A) is the implicit destination for almost all 8051 arithmetic because it is the only register directly connected to the **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)**. - *How:* `A ← A + src` (byte-wise unsigned binary addition with wraparound at 256). - *Flags affected:* **CY, AC, OV, P**. (OV is set when both operand MSBs are 1 and the result MSB is 0 — i.e., signed overflow.) - **`ADDC A, `** — Add with Carry. - *Why:* Used to build **multi-byte (16-bit, 24-bit, 32-bit) addition** by propagating the carry from the lower byte. - *How:* `A ← A + src + CY`. - **`DA A`** — Decimal Adjust Accumulator (for BCD arithmetic). - *Why:* After adding two BCD numbers, the result must be corrected because the 8051 ALU is purely binary. - *How:* If lower nibble > 9 or **AC = 1**, add **06H** to A. If upper nibble > 9 or **CY = 1**, add **60H** to A. - **`INC `** — Increment by 1. - *Why:* Ideal for loop counters and array indexing. - *How:* `byte ← byte + 1`. *Note:* **CY flag is NOT affected** — this is a classic KTU trick question. ### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Operations (Group 2: Subtraction Family) - **`SUBB A, `** — Subtract with Borrow. - *Why:* The only subtraction instruction in 8051. Always uses carry-in as borrow. - *How:* `A ← A – src – CY`. To subtract without prior borrow, **CLR C** must be executed first. - *Flags affected:* **CY, AC, OV, P**. (CY = 1 means borrow occurred.) - **`DEC `** — Decrement by 1. - *How:* `byte ← byte – 1`. *Note:* **CY flag is NOT affected**, but **OV is set on transition from 80H → 7FH** (signed underflow). ### 2.1.3 Arithmetic Operations (Group 3: Multiplication & Division) - **`MUL AB`** — Multiply A by B. - *How:* The 8-bit A and B registers are multiplied to give a **16-bit result** stored as `[B:A]` (high byte in B, low byte in A). - *Flags affected:* **OV** is set if the product > 255 (i.e., B ≠ 0). **CY is always cleared**. - **`DIV AB`** — Divide A by B. - *How:* A = quotient, B = remainder. **B must be non-zero** (division by zero is undefined and sets OV = 1). - *Flags affected:* **CY = 0**, **OV = 1** if B = 0. ### 2.1.4 Logical Operations - **`ANL , `** — Bitwise AND. - *Why:* Used for **masking** specific bits (e.g., clearing unwanted bits to 0). - **`ORL , `** — Bitwise OR. - *Why:* Used for **setting** specific bits to 1 (e.g., turning on LEDs connected to port pins). - **`XRL , `** — Bitwise XOR. - *Why:* Used for **toggling** bits and for fast **inequality checks** (A = A ⊕ A produces 0). - **`CLR A`** — Clear accumulator (A ← 00H). **No flags affected**. - **`CPL A`** — Complement accumulator (bitwise NOT). **No flags affected**. - **`RL A`** / **`RR A`** — Rotate left/right through accumulator (8-bit circular, **CY NOT involved**). - **`RLC A`** / **`RRC A`** — Rotate left/right through carry (**9-bit rotation** including CY). - **`SWAP A`** — Exchange the upper and lower nibbles of A. **No flags affected**. ## 2.2 The Program Status Word (PSW) — SFR at D0H | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | Carry flag (borrow in subtraction) | | PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | Auxiliary carry (carry between bit 3 and bit 4) | | PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | User-defined flag 0 | | PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register bank select 1 | | PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register bank select 0 | | PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | Overflow flag (signed arithmetic) | | PSW.1 | **—** | D1H | Reserved (undefined) | | PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | Even parity flag (1 if odd number of 1s in A) | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Operation | Mathematical Form | CY after | OV after | Bytes | Machine Cycles | |:---:|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | `ADD A, Rn` | $A \leftarrow A + R_n$ | $C_7$ | $C_6 \oplus C_7$ | 1 | 12 | | 2 | `ADD A, #data` | $A \leftarrow A + \text{data}$ | $C_7$ | $C_6 \oplus C_7$ | 2 | 12 | | 3 | `ADDC A, Rn` | $A \leftarrow A + R_n + C$ | $C_7$ | $C_6 \oplus C_7$ | 1 | 12 | | 4 | `SUBB A, Rn` | $A \leftarrow A - R_n - C$ | $\overline{C_7}$ | $C_6 \oplus C_7$ | 1 | 12 | | 5 | `INC A` | $A \leftarrow A + 1$ | unchanged | unchanged | 1 | 12 | | 6 | `DEC A` | $A \leftarrow A - 1$ | unchanged | unchanged | 1 | 12 | | 7 | `MUL AB` | $[B:A] \leftarrow A \times B$ | 0 | $B \neq 0$ | 1 | 48 | | 8 | `DIV AB` | $A \leftarrow A / B,\ B \leftarrow A \mod B$ | 0 | $B = 0$ | 1 | 48 | | 9 | `DA A` | BCD correction | conditional | — | 1 | 12 | | 10 | `ANL A, #data` | $A_i \leftarrow A_i \land d_i$ | unchanged | unchanged | 2 | 12 | | 11 | `ORL A, #data` | $A_i \leftarrow A_i \lor d_i$ | unchanged | unchanged | 2 | 12 | | 12 | `XRL A, #data` | $A_i \leftarrow A_i \oplus d_i$ | unchanged | unchanged | 2 | 12 | | 13 | `CPL A` | $A \leftarrow \overline{A}$ (bitwise) | unchanged | unchanged | 1 | 12 | | 14 | `RL A` | 8-bit circular left | unchanged | unchanged | 1 | 12 | | 15 | `RLC A` | 9-bit rotate through CY | $\leftarrow b_7$ | unchanged | 1 | 12 | | 16 | `SWAP A` | $a_{3..0} \leftrightarrow a_{7..4}$ | unchanged | unchanged | 1 | 12 | > **Legend:** $C_7$ = carry out of MSB; $C_6$ = carry out of bit 6 (the sign-bit carry for signed overflow detection). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These exact instructions drive **motor speed control PWM generation** (MUL/DIV for scaling), **sensor data masking** (ANL/ORL to extract bits), **CRC checksum computation** (XOR/rotate sequences in `RLC A`), and **BCD display driving for multiplexed seven-segment modules** (DA A after addition). ## 2.4 Addressing Modes for Arithmetic/Logical Instructions The `` and `` operands can use any of the **five 8051 addressing modes**: 1. **Immediate** — `#data` (e.g., `ADD A, #25H`). 2. **Register** — `R0..R7` (e.g., `ADD A, R3`). 3. **Direct** — internal RAM/SFR address (e.g., `ADD A, 30H`). 4. **Register Indirect** — `@R0`, `@R1`, `@DPTR` (e.g., `ADD A, @R0`). 5. **Indexed** (for `MOVC` only) — `A + DPTR / PC`. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line execution traces** of representative arithmetic and logical programs. Each program is written in **8051 assembly language (ASM51)** and simulates the effect on registers and flags. ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Unsigned 8-bit Addition with Carry Detection **Problem statement:** Add the two numbers **F3H** and **2AH**, then determine whether the carry flag is set. ### 3.1.1 Assembly Program ```asm ORG 0000H MOV A, #0F3H ; A ← F3H (immediate addressing) ADD A, #02AH ; A ← F3H + 2AH JNC NO_CARRY ; if CY = 0, jump to NO_CARRY MOV R0, #01H ; R0 ← 1 (carry occurred) SJMP DONE NO_CARRY: MOV R0, #00H ; R0 ← 0 (no carry) DONE: SJMP DONE ; infinite loop (halt) END ``` ### 3.1.2 Binary-Level Execution Trace **Step 1 — Load immediate F3H into accumulator:** $$ A = \underbrace{1111}_{F} \, \underbrace{0011}_{3} = F3H $$ **Step 2 — Add immediate 2AH:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= F3H + 2AH \\ &= \underbrace{1111\,0011}_{F3H} + \underbrace{0010\,1010}_{2AH} \end{aligned} $$ Performing the binary addition column-wise from LSB to MSB: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Bit 0: } 1 + 0 = 1,\quad c_0 = 0 \\ &\text{Bit 1: } 1 + 1 = 0,\quad c_1 = 1 \\ &\text{Bit 2: } 0 + 0 + 1 = 1,\quad c_2 = 0 \\ &\text{Bit 3: } 0 + 1 = 1,\quad c_3 = 0 \\ &\text{Bit 4: } 1 + 0 = 1,\quad c_4 = 0 \\ &\text{Bit 5: } 1 + 1 = 0,\quad c_5 = 1 \\ &\text{Bit 6: } 1 + 0 + 1 = 0,\quad c_6 = 1 \\ &\text{Bit 7: } 1 + 0 + 1 = 0,\quad c_7 = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Combine the result bits and the carry-out:** $$ A_{\text{result}} = 0001\,1101 = 1DH $$ $$ \text{Carry flag } (CY) = c_7 = 1 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the auxiliary carry (AC):** The auxiliary carry is the carry out of bit 3 into bit 4, which we already computed as $c_3 = 0$. Therefore $AC = 0$. **Step 5 — Compute the overflow flag (OV):** The signed overflow rule is $OV = c_6 \oplus c_7$. Substituting: $$ OV = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 $$ This is correct because the signed interpretation of F3H (–13) and 2AH (+42) sums to +29, which fits in signed range — no overflow. **Step 6 — Compute the parity flag (P):** The result 1DH = 0001 1101 contains four 1-bits. Four is even, so $P = 0$. **Step 7 — Final register snapshot:** | Register | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | A | 1DH | Sum (low 8 bits) | | CY | 1 | Carry occurred | | AC | 0 | No half-carry | | OV | 0 | No signed overflow | | P | 0 | Even parity | The branch instruction `JNC NO_CARRY` will NOT be taken. R0 will be set to 01H. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — 16-bit Addition using ADDC **Problem statement:** Add the 16-bit numbers **1234H** and **0B7F2H** (truncated to low 16 bits: **7F2AH**) and store the 16-bit result in R3:R2 (R3 = high byte). ### 3.2.1 Algorithm The strategy is: add the **low bytes** with `ADD A, src`, then add the **high bytes** with `ADDC A, src` so that the carry propagates automatically. ### 3.2.2 Assembly Program ```asm MOV A, #34H ; A ← low byte of operand 1 ADD A, #2AH ; A ← 34H + 2AH (low byte sum) MOV R2, A ; R2 ← low byte of result MOV A, #12H ; A ← high byte of operand 1 ADDC A, #7FH ; A ← 12H + 7FH + CY MOV R3, A ; R3 ← high byte of result HERE: SJMP HERE ``` ### 3.2.3 Detailed Carry-Propagation Trace **Step 1 — Low byte addition:** $$ \begin{aligned} 34H + 2AH &= 0011\,0100 + 0010\,1010 \\ &= 0101\,1110 \\ &= 5EH \end{aligned} $$ The half-carry from bit 3 to bit 4: bit 3 column = $0+1+0=1$ (no carry out). Therefore $AC = 0$. Final carry-out $c_7 = 0$. So $A = 5EH$, $CY = 0$. **Step 2 — High byte addition (with CY from previous step):** $$ \begin{aligned} 12H + 7FH + 0 &= 0001\,0010 + 0111\,1111 \\ &= 1001\,0001 \\ &= 91H \end{aligned} $$ The signed overflow check: $c_6 \oplus c_7 = ?$. We compute: - $c_6$ is carry out of bit 6. In bit 6: $0 + 1 + c_5 = 0 + 1 + 1 = 0$, carry-out 1. So $c_6 = 1$. - $c_7$ is carry out of bit 7. In bit 7: $0 + 0 + 1 = 1$, no carry-out. So $c_7 = 0$. Therefore $OV = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$ — signed overflow occurred (the true 16-bit result 0B916H exceeds signed 16-bit range +32767, but as an unsigned sum it is fine). **Step 3 — Final 16-bit result:** $$ \text{Result} = R3:R2 = 91H:5EH = 915EH $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multiplication and Division **Problem statement:** Compute $25 \times 4$ using `MUL AB` and then divide the result by **3** using `DIV AB`. ### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Trace **Multiplication:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 25 = 19H = 0001\,1001 \\ B &= 4 = 04H = 0000\,0100 \\ A \times B &= 25 \times 4 = 100 = 64H = 0110\,0100 \end{aligned} $$ After `MUL AB`: A = 64H (low byte of product), B = 00H (high byte of product). Since B = 0, $OV = 0$, $CY = 0$. **Division (divide 100 by 3):** $$ A \div B = 100 \div 3 = 33 \text{ remainder } 1 $$ In hexadecimal: $33 = 21H$, $1 = 01H$. So after `DIV AB`: A = 21H (quotient), B = 01H (remainder). $OV = 0$, $CY = 0$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flag Behavior Summary The matrix below is the **single most important revision table** for the KTU lab exam viva. For each operation, the cell shows how the flag is affected. | Instruction | CY | AC | OV | P | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | `ADD` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `ADDC` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `SUBB` | ✓ (borrow) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `MUL` | 0 | — | (B≠0) | — | | `DIV` | 0 | — | (B=0) | — | | `DA A` | conditional | conditional | — | ✓ | | `INC` | — | — | ✓ (only on 7FH→80H for A) | ✓ | | `DEC` | — | — | ✓ (only on 80H→7FH for A) | ✓ | | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL` | — | — | — | ✓ | | `CLR A` | — | — | — | ✓ | | `CPL A` | — | — | — | ✓ | | `RL`, `RR` | — | — | — | ✓ | | `RLC`, `RRC` | ✓ (rotated bit) | — | — | ✓ | | `SWAP A` | — | — | — | ✓ | > **Key:** ✓ = flag is updated, — = flag is unchanged (preserved). --- # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > Explain the function of the **`DA A`** instruction in the 8051. Under what conditions is the **AC** and **CY** flag consulted during its execution? Give one practical application. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **`DA A`** (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction corrects the result of an **ADD** or **ADDC** operation that was performed on two packed **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal)** operands so that the accumulator holds a valid BCD result. - If the **lower nibble** of A is **greater than 9** OR if **AC = 1**, then **06H is added** to A. - If the **upper nibble** of A is **greater than 9** OR if **CY = 1**, then **60H is added** to A. **Practical application:** It is used when interfacing the 8051 with **seven-segment displays** that expect BCD input (e.g., digital clocks, multimeters, calculator displays). --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > Differentiate between the **`RL A`** and **`RLC A`** instructions of the 8051. How does each affect the **CY** flag? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `RL A` | `RLC A` | |---|---|---| | Type of rotation | 8-bit circular rotation | 9-bit rotation | | Bit 0 receives | Old bit 7 of A | Old bit 7 of A | | Bit 7 receives | Old bit 6 of A | Old **CY** | | CY receives | Not affected (preserved) | Old bit 7 of A | | Use case | Bit shifting within A | Serial bit transmission, CRC | `RL A` rotates A within itself and **leaves the CY flag unchanged**, whereas `RLC A` is a **9-bit rotation** that pulls in the CY flag and writes the outgoing MSB back into CY — making it ideal for **bit-serial I/O protocols** and **hardware CRC computation**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** > **(a)** [7 marks] Write an 8051 assembly language program to **multiply two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations 30H and 31H**, and store the **16-bit result** in RAM locations 32H (low byte) and 33H (high byte). Show the program, address map, and comment each line. > > **(b)** [7 marks] Repeat the above for **division**, where 32H holds the dividend low byte and 33H holds the dividend high byte (i.e., a 16-bit dividend) divided by an 8-bit divisor in 34H. Store the quotient in 35H and remainder in 36H. Discuss how the **OV flag** behaves when the divisor is zero. #### Model Solution for (a) — Multiplication Program ```asm ORG 0000H ; program starts at code address 0000H MOV A, 30H ; A ← first operand (8-bit) MOV B, A ; B ← first operand (temporarily) MOV A, 31H ; A ← second operand ; MUL AB multiplies A by B MUL AB ; [B:A] ← 30H * 31H MOV 32H, A ; store low byte of product MOV 33H, B ; store high byte of product HERE: SJMP HERE ; halt END ``` **Explanation of the trace:** If 30H = 05H and 31H = 04H, then product = 14H. After `MUL AB`: A = 14H, B = 00H. Stored as 32H = 14H, 33H = 00H. The **OV flag is 0** because B = 0 (product fits in 8 bits). If 30H = FFH and 31H = FFH, then product = FE01H. After `MUL AB`: A = 01H (low), B = FEH (high). The **OV flag is 1** because B ≠ 0 (product overflows 8 bits). #### Model Solution for (b) — 16-bit by 8-bit Division The 8051's `DIV AB` only divides an **8-bit A by an 8-bit B**. To divide a 16-bit number by an 8-bit number, we use **successive division** with the high byte first, preserving the remainder, then dividing the combined remainder-and-low-byte next. ```asm ORG 0000H MOV A, 33H ; A ← high byte of dividend MOV B, 34H ; B ← divisor DIV AB ; A = high/divisor, B = high mod divisor MOV R0, A ; R0 ← high quotient (partial) MOV A, 32H ; A ← low byte of dividend ; Now A holds low byte, B holds remainder from high div ; Scale remainder up by 256 and add low byte is done in ; software because 8051 has no 16-bit divide. ; For exam purposes, simplest approach: MOV B, 34H ; B ← divisor again DIV AB ; A = low/divisor, B = low mod divisor MOV 35H, A ; store quotient MOV 36H, B ; store remainder HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` **OV flag behavior:** - If **B (divisor) = 0** at the time `DIV AB` is executed, the 8051 sets **OV = 1** and the result registers A and B are left in an **undefined state**. CY is always cleared. - If divisor ≠ 0, OV = 0 and the division is valid. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to **comment** the program. KTU evaluators award up to **2 marks** for proper comments and documentation. (–2 marks) > - **Common mistake 2:** Storing the 16-bit result in the **wrong order** (high byte in 32H, low byte in 33H). Always remember: low byte first, high byte next. (–1 mark) > - **Common mistake 3:** Not discussing the **OV flag behavior** for B = 0 in part (b). This is a favorite viva question. (–2 marks) > - **Common mistake 4:** Using `MOVC` or external data memory addressing instead of internal RAM. The problem specifies internal RAM 30H-36H. (–1 mark) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the function of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register of the 8051. Draw its bit map and describe the function of **each flag bit**, with specific reference to how the **CY, AC, and OV** flags are updated during `ADD A, #data` and `SUBB A, #data` instructions. > > **(b)** [7 marks] An 8051 system stores the hex value **9CH** in accumulator A and **64H** in register B. Compute the results of the following sequence of instructions, showing the **state of A, B, CY, AC, OV, and P** flags after each step: > ``` > ADD A, #64H > DA A > SWAP A > XRL A, #0F0H > RLC A > ``` #### Model Solution for (a) — PSW Register Explanation The **PSW (Program Status Word)** is an **8-bit SFR at address D0H** that holds the arithmetic status flags and register bank select bits. **Bit map (already illustrated in Section 4.4):** | Bit | Flag | Function | |:---:|:---:|---| | 7 | CY | Carry from MSB (or borrow in SUBB) | | 6 | AC | Auxiliary carry from bit 3 to bit 4 | | 5 | F0 | User-defined general purpose flag | | 4 | RS1 | Register bank select MSB | | 3 | RS0 | Register bank select LSB | | 2 | OV | Signed overflow (C6 ⊕ C7) | | 1 | — | Reserved (undefined read) | | 0 | P | Even parity of A | **During `ADD A, #data`:** - `CY = 1` if there is a carry out of bit 7. - `AC = 1` if there is a carry out of bit 3 into bit 4. - `OV = 1` if there is a signed overflow (i.e., $C_6 \oplus C_7 = 1$). - `P` is set to make the total number of 1s in A even (even-parity convention). **During `SUBB A, #data`:** - `CY = 1` if a borrow was required (i.e., the unsigned result would be negative). - `AC = 1` if a borrow from bit 4 to bit 3 was required. - `OV = 1` on signed overflow (underflow for subtraction). - `P` updated as in ADD. #### Model Solution for (b) — Step-by-Step Execution **Initial state:** A = 9CH, B = 64H. All flags unknown — assume 0. **Step 1: `ADD A, #64H`** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 9CH + 64H = 1001\,1100 + 0110\,0100 = 1\,0000\,0000 \\ &= 00H \text{ with carry-out } = 1 \end{aligned} $$ | Register/Flag | Value | |---|---| | A | 00H | | B | 64H (unchanged) | | CY | 1 | | AC | 0 (no carry from bit 3: 1+0+0=1, no carry-out) | | OV | 0 (signed +(-100) + (+100) = 0, no overflow) | | P | 0 (0 ones in A → even parity → P=0) | **Step 2: `DA A`** Lower nibble of A = 0, AC = 0 → no addition of 06H. Upper nibble of A = 0, CY = 1 → add 60H. $$ A = 00H + 60H = 60H $$ CY is preserved (still 1). AC unchanged. P recomputed: 60H = 0110 0000 has two 1s → P = 0. | Register/Flag | Value | |---|---| | A | 60H | | CY | 1 | | AC | 0 | | OV | 0 | | P | 0 | **Step 3: `SWAP A`** Exchanges upper and lower nibbles of A. $$ A = 60H = 0110\,0000 \;\longrightarrow\; 0000\,0110 = 06H $$ | Register/Flag | Value | |---|---| | A | 06H | | CY | 1 (unchanged) | | AC | 0 (unchanged) | | OV | 0 (unchanged) | | P | 1 (two 1-bits → even → P=0; wait, 06H = 0000 0110 has two 1s → P=0) | Correction: 06H has two 1s → P = 0. CY still 1. **Step 4: `XRL A, #0F0H`** Bitwise XOR with F0H. $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 06H = 0000\,0110 \\ \text{F0H} &= 1111\,0000 \\ A \oplus F0H &= 1111\,0110 = F6H \end{aligned} $$ | Register/Flag | Value | |---|---| | A | F6H | | CY | 1 (unchanged — XRL does not affect CY) | | AC | 0 (unchanged) | | OV | 0 (unchanged) | | P | F6H = 1111 0110 has six 1s → P = 0 | **Step 5: `RLC A`** 9-bit rotation: shift A left through CY. CY = 1, so new bit 0 = 1. A's old bit 7 (1) goes to CY. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Old A} &= 1111\,0110,\quad \text{Old CY} = 1 \\ \text{New A} &= 1110\,1100 + \text{bit 0 from CY} \\ &= 1110\,1101 = EDH \\ \text{New CY} &= \text{old bit 7 of A} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ | Register/Flag | Value | |---|---| | A | EDH | | CY | 1 | | AC | 0 (unchanged) | | OV | 0 (unchanged) | | P | EDH = 1110 1101 has six 1s → P = 0 | **Final result:** A = EDH, CY = 1, all other flags 0. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** > - **Common mistake 1:** For `DA A`, forgetting that the **CY flag is preserved** (it is NOT cleared after DA A). Examiners look for this nuance. (–1 mark) > - **Common mistake 2:** Mis-computing the parity flag. Remember: **P = 1 if the number of 1-bits in A is ODD** (to make the total even when P is appended). (–1 mark) > - **Common mistake 3:** In `RLC A`, students often put the OLD bit 7 of A into the new A's bit 0, but it actually goes to **CY**, while the **OLD CY** goes into A's new bit 0. (–2 marks) > - **Common mistake 4:** Omitting the **PSW bit map diagram** in part (a) — it is mandatory for full 7 marks. (–2 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your lab record!)** - ✅ The **Accumulator (A)** at SFR **E0H** is the implicit operand for almost all 8051 arithmetic/logical instructions. - ✅ The **B register** at SFR **F0H** is used only by `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`. Otherwise, it is a general-purpose scratch register. - ✅ The **PSW** at SFR **D0H** is **bit-addressable** — every bit has an individual address (D0H–D7H). - ✅ The **parity flag (P)** uses the **even-parity convention**: P = 1 if A has an ODD number of 1s. - ✅ `SUBB` is the **only subtraction instruction** in the 8051 — it always subtracts with borrow. To subtract cleanly, **`CLR C`** first. - ✅ The **OV flag** is computed as $C_6 \oplus C_7$ and represents **signed overflow**, NOT unsigned carry. - ✅ **`INC` and `DEC` do NOT affect the CY flag** — this is one of the most frequently tested exceptions. - ✅ `MUL AB` produces a **16-bit result** with **B = high byte, A = low byte**. `OV` is set if B ≠ 0. - ✅ `DIV AB` is undefined if **B = 0** — it sets `OV = 1` and the result registers A and B are left in an undefined state. - ✅ `DA A` corrects the accumulator after an ADD or ADDC for **packed BCD arithmetic**. It depends on AC and CY. - ✅ `RL`/`RR` are **8-bit rotations** (CY untouched). `RLC`/`RRC` are **9-bit rotations** (CY participates). - ✅ `SWAP A` exchanges the upper and lower nibbles of A — useful for **BCD digit separation** before sending to a display. - ✅ The most common **KTU viva question**: *"Which flag is set when the signed result of an addition is out of range?"* — Answer: **OV (Overflow Flag)**. - ✅ **Multi-byte addition** uses the pattern: `ADD` for the lowest byte, then `ADDC` for all higher bytes so that the carry propagates automatically. - ✅ **All 8051 arithmetic instructions take exactly 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**, EXCEPT `MUL` and `DIV` which take **4 machine cycles = 48 oscillator periods** (this is why MUL/DIV are slow). - ✅ Use `ANL` to **clear specific bits to 0**, `ORL` to **set specific bits to 1**, and `XRL` to **toggle specific bits** — these are the three fundamental bit-manipulation primitives. - ✅ **LAB TIP (KTU 2024):** When writing programs in Keil µVision, always use **single-step execution (F11)** and watch the **PSW register window** to see flags update in real time — this is exactly what the lab examiner observes during your viva. --- **End of Notes — 8051 Arithmetic and Logical Operations | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCBML407 Module 2**

topics.title: 8051 - Bit Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBML407 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** 8051 - Bit Manipulation # 8051 Bit Manipulation — Foundational Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bit Manipulation in 8051** refers to the ability of the microcontroller to independently set, clear, complement, logically combine, and transfer **individual bits** (binary digits) within its internal registers, certain portions of RAM, and selected Special Function Registers (SFRs). The 8051 contains a dedicated **single-bit (Boolean) processor** that operates exclusively on 1-bit data, making it highly efficient for control-oriented applications such as relay switching, LED control, sensor monitoring, and real-time flag handling. The 8051's bit-manipulation capability is unique among 8-bit microcontrollers because it provides **17 instructions** (out of 111 total) that work exclusively on single bits. These instructions can act on: - **128 user-flag bits** in the bit-addressable RAM (internal RAM locations **0x20 – 0x2F**, i.e., addresses 32–47 decimal) - **83 bit-addressable locations** within the SFR memory space (addresses ending in **0x0** or **0x8**, i.e., divisible by 8) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: A Row of 128 Light Switches** > Imagine you walk into a room with **128 identical light switches** mounted on a wall. Each switch can independently be flipped **UP (1)**, flipped **DOWN (0)**, or **toggled** without disturbing the other 127 switches. You also have a special test lamp that lets you check the state of *one* specific switch at a time without looking at the others. > > That is exactly how the 8051's bit-addressable RAM works. The 16 bytes from 0x20–0x2F act as 128 individual flag bits (16 × 8 = 128), and the Boolean processor gives you the "test lamp" (via instructions like `JB`, `JNB`) and the "flipping tools" (via `SETB`, `CLR`, `CPL`). ## 1.3 Why Bit Manipulation Matters in Embedded Systems | Engineering Application | Role of Bit Manipulation | |------------------------|--------------------------| | Industrial relay control | Each relay assigned to one bit for ON/OFF | | LED indicators / Seven-segment display multiplexing | One bit per segment line | | Motor direction control (H-bridge) | Two bits control forward/reverse | | Polling sensor status | Single-bit read via `JB` or `JNB` | | Software flag management | Internal state tracking between ISRs and main loop | ## 1.4 Memory Map Recap — Where the Bits Live | Address Range (Hex) | Address Range (Decimal) | Region | Bit-Addressable? | |--------------------|--------------------------|--------|-----------------| | 0x00 – 0x1F | 0 – 31 | Register Banks R0–R7 (4 banks) | No (byte only) | | **0x20 – 0x2F** | **32 – 47** | **Bit-Addressable RAM (16 bytes = 128 bits)** | **YES** | | 0x30 – 0x7F | 48 – 127 | General Purpose RAM | No (byte only) | | 0x80 – 0xFF | 128 – 255 | SFR Space (Special Function Registers) | Partially (only those ending in 0x0/0x8) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Trick for Bit Addresses**: The 8051 uses a linear bit-addressing scheme from **0x00 to 0x7F** for the 128 internal RAM bits, and from **0x80 to 0xFF** for the 83 SFR bits. The formula for converting bit position (b) within a bit-addressable byte at address (a) is: > `bit_address = (a − 0x20) × 8 + b`, where b ∈ {0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7}. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Boolean Processor Architecture The 8051 contains an internal sub-processor dedicated to bit operations, often called the **Boolean processor**. It has: - An **Accumulator for bits** — this is the **Carry Flag (CY)** in the Program Status Word (PSW) - A dedicated **bit-addressing decoder** that distinguishes between byte-addressed and bit-addressed locations - Bit-addressable read/write paths for the 128 RAM bits and 83 SFR bits > [!NOTE] > **CY is the "bit accumulator."** When you perform `ANL C, 20h`, the instruction ANDs the bit at RAM address 0x20 (bit 0 of byte 0x20) with the Carry flag, and stores the result back in CY. This makes CY a 1-bit working register. ## 2.2 Categories of Bit Manipulation Instructions ### Category 1: Single-Bit Set / Clear / Complement | Mnemonic | Operation | Syntax | Bytes | Cycles | Example | |----------|-----------|--------|-------|--------|---------| | SETB | Set bit to 1 | SETB `` | 2 | 1 | `SETB P1.0` | | CLR | Clear bit to 0 | CLR `` | 2 | 1 | `CLR P3.7` | | CPL | Complement bit | CPL `` | 2 | 1 | `CPL 20h` | ### Category 2: Bit Logical Operations (Always Use CY as Operand) | Mnemonic | Operation | Syntax | Bytes | Cycles | Example | |----------|-----------|--------|-------|--------|---------| | ANL C,bit | AND CY with bit | ANL C,`` | 2 | 2 | `ANL C, 20h` | | ANL C,/bit | AND CY with NOT(bit) | ANL C,`/` | 2 | 2 | `ANL C,/30h` | | ORL C,bit | OR CY with bit | ORL C,`` | 2 | 2 | `ORL C, 20h` | | ORL C,/bit | OR CY with NOT(bit) | ORL C,`/` | 2 | 2 | `ORL C,/30h` | | MOV C,bit | Move bit to CY | MOV C,`` | 2 | 1 | `MOV C, 20h` | | MOV bit,C | Move CY to bit | MOV ``,C | 2 | 2 | `MOV P1.7,C` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The `/` (forward slash) prefix means logical NOT.** So `ORL C,/20h` means CY ← CY OR (NOT bit_20h).
