## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
Explain the role of the **STATUS register** in PIC16F877A arithmetic operations. List its flags and the instructions that affect them.

**Model Answer:**

The STATUS register (address 0x03, 0x83) is an 8-bit special function register that holds the arithmetic status of the ALU. The relevant flags for arithmetic operations are:

| Bit | Name | Function |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | **C (Carry)** | Set by `ADDWF`, `SUBWF`, `SUBLW`; indicates carry-out of bit 7 (addition) or no-borrow (subtraction) |
| 1 | **DC (Digit Carry)** | Set if carry from bit 3 to bit 4; used for **BCD correction** |
| 2 | **Z (Zero)** | Set if the result of an arithmetic or logic operation is 0x00 |
| 3 | **PD (Power Down)** | Cleared by `SLEEP` instruction |
| 4 | **TO (Time Out)** | Cleared on WDT timeout |
| 5, 6 | RP0, RP1 | Register Bank select bits |
| 7 | IRP | Indirect addressing bank select (only for PIC16F877, not 877A) |

These flags are critical for **multi-precision arithmetic** and **conditional branching** (e.g., `BTFSS STATUS, 0` to test Carry).

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
Why is the **Carry flag polarity inverted** for the `SUBWF` instruction in PIC16F877A? Illustrate with an example.

**Model Answer:**

In the PIC16F877A, subtraction is performed by **adding the 2's complement** of the subtrahend to the minuend. Mathematically:

$$A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$$

If $A \geq B$, no borrow occurs in the original subtraction, and the final carry-out of the addition will be **1** (because the result fits in 8 bits). If $A < B$, a borrow occurs, and the carry-out will be **0**.

**Example:** $A = 0x85, B = 0x40$ (computed in Section 3.2.2). Result = $0x45$ with $C = 1$, meaning **no borrow**.

Conversely, $A = 0x40, B = 0x85$ would give a borrow, and $C = 0$.

Hence, the rule is: **C = 1 → No Borrow; C = 0 → Borrow Occurred**.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]**

**(a)** Design an 8-bit **subtraction circuit** using the PIC16F877A that computes $Z = A - B$, displays the result on PORTB, and shows the borrow status on RD0. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Modify the program in (a) to handle **16-bit operands** stored in registers `0x21:0x20` (A) and `0x23:0x22` (B), producing a 16-bit result at `0x25:0x24`. Show the complete XC8 code. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Algorithm:**

1. Set `TRISB = 0x00`, `TRISD = 0x00`.
2. Load A (e.g., 0x85) into WREG.
3. Subtract B (e.g., 0x40) using `SUBWF`.
4. Display the result on PORTB.
5. Check `STATUSbits.C`:
   - If `C = 1` → no borrow → RD0 = 0.
   - If `C = 0` → borrow occurred → RD0 = 1.

**XC8 Code:**

```c
#include <xc.h>
#pragma config FOSC = HS, WDTE = OFF, PWRTE = ON, BOREN = OFF, LVP = OFF
#define _XTAL_FREQ 20000000

void main(void)
{
    TRISB = 0x00;
    TRISD = 0x00;
    PORTB = 0x00;
    PORTD = 0x00;

    unsigned char A = 0x85;
    unsigned char B = 0x40;
    unsigned char Z = 0x00;

    Z = A - B;                       // 0x45
    PORTB = Z;

    if (STATUSbits.C == 1)           // C = 1 → no borrow
        PORTDbits.RD0 = 0;
    else
        PORTDbits.RD0 = 1;           // C = 0 → borrow

    while(1);
}
```

**Verification (Proteus):** Connect 8 LEDs to PORTB and 1 LED to RD0. Run simulation; observe `PORTB = 0x45` and `RD0 = 0`.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Algorithm for 16-bit subtraction:**

The 16-bit subtraction is performed as **two 8-bit subtractions**, with the high byte subtraction using the **borrow** from the low byte.

$$Z_{H:L} = A_{H:L} - B_{H:L}$$

- Step 1: Subtract low bytes: $L_Z = A_L - B_L$. Save the borrow (inverted C).
- Step 2: Subtract high bytes: $H_Z = A_H - B_H - \text{borrow}$.

**XC8 Code:**

```c
#include <xc.h>
#pragma config FOSC = HS, WDTE = OFF, PWRTE = ON, BOREN = OFF, LVP = OFF
#define _XTAL_FREQ 20000000

void main(void)
{
    TRISB = 0x00;     // Low byte of result
    TRISC = 0x00;     // High byte of result
    TRISD = 0x00;
    PORTB = 0x00;
    PORTC = 0x00;

    unsigned char A_H = 0x12, A_L = 0x34;   // A = 0x1234 = 4660
    unsigned char B_H = 0x08, B_L = 0x56;   // B = 0x0856 = 2134
    unsigned char Z_H = 0, Z_L = 0;
    unsigned char borrow = 0;

    // Step 1: Low byte subtraction
    if (A_L >= B_L) {
        Z_L = A_L - B_L;
        borrow = 0;
    } else {
        Z_L = A_L + ((~B_L) + 1);   // 2's complement addition
        borrow = 1;
    }

    // Step 2: High byte subtraction with borrow
    if (A_H >= B_H + borrow) {
        Z_H = A_H - B_H - borrow;
    } else {
        Z_H = A_H + ((~(B_H + borrow)) + 1);
    }

    PORTB = Z_L;        // 0xDE
    PORTC = Z_H;        // 0x09
    // Result = 0x09DE = 2526 (since 4660 - 2134 = 2526)

    if (borrow)
        PORTDbits.RD0 = 1;     // Final borrow indicator
    else
        PORTDbits.RD0 = 0;

    while(1);
}
```

**Verification:** $0x1234 - 0x0856 = 0x09DE = 2526$. Expected: $4660 - 2134 = 2526$ ✓

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Design and implement a **multiplication routine** in PIC16F877A using **repeated addition** to compute the product of two 8-bit numbers $M$ and $N$. Show the algorithm and explain why a 16-bit result register is required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete **Embedded C** program for the PIC16F877A that accepts a 4-bit input from PORTA (representing a number $N$) and computes the **square** of $N$ using repeated addition, displaying the result on PORTB and PORTD. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Algorithm:**

$$\text{Product} = M \times N = \sum_{i=1}^{N} M$$

A 16-bit register is required because the maximum 8-bit × 8-bit product is $0xFF \times 0xFF = 65025$, which exceeds the 8-bit range ($0$ to $255$).

| Step | Operation | Register |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Initialize counter $i = 0$ | — |
| 2 | Load $M$ into WREG | W |
| 3 | Add $M$ to product (16-bit) | Prod_H, Prod_L |
| 4 | Increment counter $i$ | — |
| 5 | If $i < N$, go to Step 2 | — |
| 6 | Output Prod_L to PORTB, Prod_H to PORTD | — |

**Mathematical Justification for 16-bit Width:**

$$0xFF \times 0xFF = 255 \times 255 = 65025 = 0xFE01$$

This is greater than $2^8 = 256$, so it cannot fit in a single 8-bit register. A 16-bit register can hold values from $0$ to $65535$, which is sufficient.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**XC8 Code:**

```c
#include <xc.h>
#pragma config FOSC = HS, WDTE = OFF, PWRTE = ON, BOREN = OFF, LVP = OFF
#define _XTAL_FREQ 20000000

void main(void)
{
    TRISA = 0x0F;        // Lower 4 bits of PORTA as input (RA0-RA3)
    TRISB = 0x00;        // PORTB output (low byte of square)
    TRISD = 0x00;        // PORTD output (high byte of square)
    PORTB = 0x00;
    PORTD = 0x00;

    unsigned char N = 0;
    unsigned int  square = 0;

    N = PORTA & 0x0F;    // Mask lower 4 bits (N ranges 0 to 15)

    square = 0;
    for (unsigned char i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        square = square + N;    // Repeated addition
    }

    PORTB = (unsigned char)(square & 0x00FF);
    PORTD = (unsigned char)((square >> 8) & 0x00FF);

    while(1);
}
```

**Verification (Sample Test Cases):**

| $N$ (Input) | $N^2$ (Expected) | PORTB (Hex) | PORTD (Hex) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 0x00 | 0x00 |
| 5 | 25 | 0x19 | 0x00 |
| 10 | 100 | 0x64 | 0x00 |
| 15 | 225 | 0xE1 | 0x00 |
| 16 (illegal) | — | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mask PORTA** (`& 0x0F`): If the student does not mask, the upper 4 bits of PORTA (which are configured as outputs in some designs) may corrupt the value of $N$. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Not declaring `square` as `unsigned int`**: Using `unsigned char` will cause silent overflow for $N \geq 16$. The compiler will not warn, and the program will produce wrong results. **Penalty: 3 marks.**
>
> 3. **Wrong bitwise operator**: Using `&&` (logical AND) instead of `&` (bitwise AND) when masking. This is a classic C language mistake. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Missing `#define _XTAL_FREQ`**: Causes `__delay_ms()` macros to fail. Though not used here, it is a good practice. **Penalty: 1 mark** if `__delay_ms` is required in the question.
>
> 5. **Confusing Carry flag polarity in subtraction** (`C=0` means borrow in PIC): This is a recurring mistake across KTU papers. **Penalty: 2–3 marks** depending on the question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- [ ] **PIC16F877A has NO hardware MUL** – multiplication MUST be done by repeated addition in software.
- [ ] **8-bit × 8-bit product requires 16 bits** to store (max = 0xFE01 = 65025).
- [ ] **Carry flag (C) polarity in subtraction is INVERTED**: `C = 1` → no borrow; `C = 0` → borrow occurred.
- [ ] **Digit Carry (DC)** is set when carry occurs from bit 3 to bit 4 – used for BCD correction.
- [ ] **Zero flag (Z) = 1** when the result of an arithmetic operation equals 0x00.
- [ ] **TRISx register**: `1` = input, `0` = output. Forgetting to configure TRIS causes PORTS to remain in Hi-Impedance state.
- [ ] **Bank 1 (RP0 = 1)** must be selected to access TRIS registers. Bank 0 (RP0 = 0) is the default for PORT data registers.
- [ ] **Instruction cycle** = 4 × oscillator period. At 20 MHz, one instruction = 200 ns.
- [ ] **XC8 compiler** auto-inserts `ADDWF` for `+` and `SUBWF` for `-` in C code. Direct register manipulation is faster but more error-prone.
- [ ] **2's complement identity**: $-X = \overline{X} + 1$, used universally in PIC for subtraction.
- [ ] **Multi-byte addition**: Add low bytes first, propagate carry to high bytes.
- [ ] **Proteus configuration**: Always set **Clock Frequency = 20 MHz** in the PIC's properties; otherwise, delays will be wrong.
- [ ] **Compiler output file**: Use the `.hex` file from `dist/default/production/` for Proteus, not the `.cof` (which is for MPLAB simulator).
- [ ] **Watchdog Timer (WDT)**: Always disable (`WDTE = OFF`) for lab experiments; otherwise, the program may reset unexpectedly.
- [ ] **Power-On Reset**: `PWRTE = ON` is recommended to give the power supply time to stabilize.
- [ ] **Hex–Decimal Conversions** you must memorize: `0x0A = 10`, `0x64 = 100`, `0xFF = 255`, `0x100 = 256`, `0xFE01 = 65025`.
- [ ] **Order of operations in C**: Use **parentheses liberally** to avoid precedence bugs in compound expressions like `A + B * C - D`.
- [ ] **Always use `unsigned char`** for 8-bit arithmetic in PIC to avoid sign-extension bugs in the MPLAB XC8 compiler.
- [ ] **Memory Map**: GPRs start at 0x20; SFRs occupy 0x00–0x1F; do not use addresses below 0x20 for variables.
- [ ] **Configuration bits** in `#pragma config` must match the hardware (HS oscillator, no LVP, no WDT, no code protection for lab).
- [ ] **Viva Tip**: When asked "Why repeated addition?" the correct answer is *"Because PIC16F877A has an 8-bit ALU with no hardware multiplier unit (no MUL instruction), unlike PIC18F which has MULWF."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing Stepper Motor with PIC16F
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML407 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Stepper Motor with PIC16F

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepper Motor:** A *brushless, synchronous DC motor* that divides a full rotation into a number of equal discrete steps. The motor's position can be commanded to move and hold at one of these steps without any feedback sensor (open-loop control), making it ideal for microcontroller-based digital control systems.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCBML407** lab, the experiment involves driving a **unipolar stepper motor** (typically a **5-wire or 6-wire** type) using a **PIC16F877A/887** microcontroller through a current-driver IC such as the **ULN2003A** (or the **L293D** dual H-bridge for bipolar motors).

## 1.2 Key Engineering Parameters

- **Step Angle ($\theta_s$):** The angular displacement produced by the rotor per excitation pulse. Standard values: **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **7.5°** (48 steps/rev).
- **Steps per Revolution ($N$):** The total number of discrete steps in one full 360° rotation.
- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** The maximum torque the motor can sustain while energized without losing step position.
- **Step Rate ($f_s$):** Number of step pulses applied per second, measured in **steps/second** or **PPS (Pulses Per Second)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **stepper motor as a digital clock's second hand** (the one that *ticks* one second at a time rather than sweeping smoothly). Each electrical pulse you send to the motor is like one "tick" — the shaft rotates a fixed, predictable angle. Send 200 ticks and it completes one full circle. The microcontroller acts as the **conductor of an orchestra**, deciding which coils (instruments) play and in what sequence (sheet music), thereby choreographing the precise rotation.

## 1.4 Types of Stepper Motors

| Type | Construction | Rotor | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft iron rotor with salient poles | No magnet | High-speed, low-torque |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized rotor | Permanent magnet | Low-cost toys, indicators |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | Combination of PM and VR features | Magnetized toothed rotor | **Industry standard, CNC, robotics** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Interface a Stepper Motor with a Microcontroller?

A stepper motor requires **sequenced energization** of its stator coils in a specific order. Doing this manually with switches is impractical. A microcontroller (PIC16F) generates the precise **timing pattern** through software, allowing:
- **Direction control** (CW/CCW) by reversing the sequence.
- **Speed control** by varying the delay between steps.
- **Angle control** by counting the exact number of pulses.

## 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. The **PIC16F** outputs a 4-bit binary pattern on a port (e.g., **PORTB** or **PORTD**).
2. The 4-bit pattern is fed into the **ULN2003A** Darlington driver array.
3. The **ULN2003A** amplifies the logic-level signals (0–5 V) to the higher current (~500 mA per channel) required by the motor coils.
4. Each output of the ULN2003A drives one coil (A, B, C, D) of the unipolar stepper motor in a specific sequence.
5. Software delay between steps determines the **rotational speed**.

## 2.3 Stepping Sequences (The Core Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most commonly tested sequence in KTU exams is the **4-step full-step sequence (1-phase ON)** and the **8-step half-step sequence**.

### A. Full-Step Sequence (1-Phase ON) — Wave Drive
| Step | Coil A (RB0) | Coil B (RB1) | Coil C (RB2) | Coil D (RB3) | Hex Value |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 |

### B. Full-Step Sequence (2-Phase ON) — Full-Torque Drive
| Step | Coil A | Coil B | Coil C | Coil D | Hex Value |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 |

### C. Half-Step Sequence (8 Steps) — Higher Resolution
| Step | A | B | C | D | Hex |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 |
| 8 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step Angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$ | degrees (°) | $N$ = steps per revolution |
| Steps per Revolution | $N = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\theta_s}$ | steps | For $1.8^\circ$ motor, $N = 200$ |
| Step Rate | $f_s = \dfrac{\text{RPM} \times N}{60}$ | steps/sec (PPS) | Pulses per second |
| Delay between steps | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{60}{\text{RPM} \times N}$ | seconds | Use for software delay loops |
| Shaft angle for $k$ steps | $\theta_{\text{shaft}} = k \cdot \theta_s$ | degrees | Linear relationship |
| Resolution improvement (half-step) | $\theta_{s,\text{half}} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{2}$ | degrees | Halves the step angle |
| Timer preload (PIC16F, $f_{\text{osc}}=20$ MHz) | $T_{\text{inst}} = \dfrac{4}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds | Instruction cycle = 4 clock cycles |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machines & 3D Printers:** Precise X-Y-Z axis positioning without encoders.
- **Robotics:** Wheel odometry, joint control in robotic arms.
- **Hard Disk Drives:** Head positioning actuator.
- **Printers & Plotters:** Paper feed and print-head positioning.
- **Automotive:** Instrument cluster gauges, adaptive headlamp levelling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle from Mechanical Construction

A stepper motor has a stator with a number of pole pairs and a rotor with a number of teeth. The relationship governing the step angle is derived from the requirement that the rotor must advance by **one tooth pitch** for each electrical step.

Let:
- $N_s$ = number of stator pole pairs
- $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth
- $\theta_s$ = step angle (mechanical, in degrees)

The mechanical angle subtended by one rotor tooth pitch on the stator circumference is:

$$
\theta_{\text{tooth}} = \frac{360^\circ}{N_r}
$$

Since energizing one phase moves the rotor by $\dfrac{1}{N_s}$ of a tooth pitch (to align with the nearest stator pole), the step angle is:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{N_s \cdot N_r}
$$

**Example:** For a motor with $N_s = 50$ stator pole pairs and $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth (a typical configuration):

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{50 \times 50} = \frac{360^\circ}{2500} = 0.144^\circ
$$

This gives $N = \dfrac{360^\circ}{0.144^\circ} = 2500$ steps per revolution (typical of high-resolution hybrid motors).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Computing the Required Software Delay for a Given RPM

**Problem:** A 1.8° stepper motor is required to rotate at **30 RPM** in the clockwise direction. Calculate the delay that must be inserted between consecutive step pulses in the PIC16F program.

**Step 1 — Identify steps per revolution:**

$$
N = \frac{360^\circ}{\theta_s} = \frac{360^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate steps per second (PPS):**

$$
f_s = \frac{\text{RPM} \times N}{60} = \frac{30 \times 200}{60} = \frac{6000}{60} = 100 \text{ steps/sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to delay time between steps:**

$$
T_d = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to instruction cycles (PIC16F, $f_{\text{osc}} = 20$ MHz):**

$$
T_{\text{inst}} = \frac{4}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{4}{20 \times 10^6} = 0.2 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

$$
\text{Number of instruction cycles for 10 ms} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — For a single `__delay_ms()` macro in XC8:**

Use `__delay_ms(10)` directly, or build a nested loop if generating manual delay.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Computing Shaft Angle for a Given Number of Pulses

**Problem:** A 7.5° stepper motor is given **96 step pulses**. What is the total angular rotation of the shaft?

$$
\theta_{\text{shaft}} = k \cdot \theta_s = 96 \times 7.5^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{shaft}} = 720^\circ
$$

Since $720^\circ = 2 \times 360^\circ$, the shaft completes **2 full revolutions**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting Drive Mode for Application

| Application Requirement | Recommended Drive Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum holding torque | 2-phase ON (Full-step) | Both adjacent coils energized → strongest detent |
| Maximum angular resolution | Half-step (8-step) | Step angle halved → smoother motion |
| Minimum power consumption | 1-phase ON (Wave drive) | Only one coil energized at a time |
| Smooth motion at low speed | Microstepping (with PWM + L297/L298) | Sinusoidal coil currents → near-continuous rotation |
| High speed (above 1000 PPS) | Full-step 2-phase | Avoids resonance issues of half-step |

## 3.5 Verifiable Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Given:** A unipolar stepper motor with **48 steps/rev** is driven through ULN2003 by PIC16F877A. The sequence table in the program uses **half-stepping** with a delay of **5 ms** per step.
>
> **Find:** (a) The step angle in half-step mode. (b) The rotational speed in RPM. (c) The time for one full revolution.

**(a) Full-step angle:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{48} = 7.5^\circ
$$

Half-step angle:

$$
\theta_{s,\text{half}} = \frac{7.5^\circ}{2} = 3.75^\circ
$$

**(b) Steps per second:**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_d} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ steps/sec}
$$

In half-step mode, effective steps per revolution:

$$
N_{\text{half}} = 2 \times 48 = 96 \text{ steps}
$$

Speed in RPM:

$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{f_s \times 60}{N_{\text{half}}} = \frac{200 \times 60}{96} = 125 \text{ RPM}
$$

**(c) Time for one full revolution:**

$$
T_{\text{rev}} = \frac{N_{\text{half}}}{f_s} = \frac{96}{200} = 0.48 \text{ s} = 480 \text{ ms}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Architecture: PIC16F ↔ Stepper Motor

The diagram below maps the **functional signal flow** from the microcontroller to the motor shaft, including the current-amplification stage and the coil-energization logic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwrDC[DC Power Supply 5V 12V] --> picVCC
    pic[PIC16F877A Microcontroller] -->|4 bit logic signals| uln[ULN2003A Driver IC]
    pwrDC --> uln
    uln -->|High current drive A| coilA[Stepper Coil A]
    uln -->|High current drive B| coilB[Stepper Coil B]
    uln -->|High current drive C| coilC[Stepper Coil C]
    uln -->|High current drive D| coilD[Stepper Coil D]
    coilA --> motor[Stepper Motor Shaft]
    coilB --> motor
    coilC --> motor
    coilD --> motor
    motor -->|Mechanical rotation| load[Mechanical Load]
    osc[Crystal Oscillator 20MHz] --> pic
    picVCC[5V Regulator 7805] --> pic
```

## 4.2 Program Execution Flow (Step-Sequence Generator)

The flowchart below illustrates how the PIC16F firmware iterates through the step sequence with timed delays to produce continuous rotation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> init[Initialize PORTB as Output]
    init --> setDir[Set TRISB to 0x00]
    setDir --> table[Load Step Table in ROM]
    table --> load[Read Current Step Value]
    load --> outport[Write Value to PORTB]
    outport --> delay[Software Delay Td ms]
    delay --> check{Step Index}
    check -->|Index 0 to 2| incr[Increment Index]
    check -->|Index 3| reset[Reset Index to 0]
    incr --> load
    reset --> load
    load -->|Infinite loop| delay
```

## 4.3 Coil-Activation Timing Diagram (2-Phase Full-Step, CW)

The state diagram below shows the energization pattern for **clockwise rotation** in the 2-phase full-step mode over four discrete time slots.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> step1
    step1: STEP 1 - Coils A B ON - Hex 0x0C
    step1 --> step2
    step2: STEP 2 - Coils B C ON - Hex 0x06
    step2 --> step3
    step3: STEP 3 - Coils C D ON - Hex 0x03
    step3 --> step4
    step4: STEP 4 - Coils D A ON - Hex 0x09
    step4 --> step1
```

## 4.4 Hardware Pin Map (Typical Configuration)

| PIC16F Pin | ULN2003A Pin | Motor Coil | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| RB0 (Port B bit 0) | IN1 | Coil A | Phase A drive |
| RB1 (Port B bit 1) | IN2 | Coil B | Phase B drive |
| RB2 (Port B bit 2) | IN3 | Coil C | Phase C drive |
| RB3 (Port B bit 3) | IN4 | Coil D | Phase D drive |
| +5V (VDD) | COM | VCC | Logic supply to ULN2003 |
| +12V (External) | — | Motor VCC | Motor torque supply |
| GND (VSS) | — | GND | Common reference |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define step angle and steps per revolution for a stepper motor. A stepper motor has a step angle of 7.5°. Calculate its steps per revolution.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **step angle** is the minimum angular displacement the rotor moves for a single step pulse. **Steps per revolution** is the number of steps required to complete one full 360° rotation.
>
> $$
> N = \frac{360^\circ}{\theta_s} = \frac{360^\circ}{7.5^\circ} = 48 \text{ steps/revolution}
> $$

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is a driver IC (ULN2003) required between a PIC microcontroller and a stepper motor? Can the motor be driven directly from the PIC I/O pin?**

**Model Answer:**

> A PIC16F I/O pin can source or sink only about **25 mA** maximum, which is far below the **200–500 mA** required by a stepper motor coil. The **ULN2003A** is a **Darlington transistor array** that:
> - Amplifies the **logic-level** (0–5 V) signals from the PIC to the higher current/voltage needed by the motor coils.
> - Provides **isolation** between the low-power digital circuit and the inductive motor load.
> - Includes **freewheeling diodes** internally to protect against back-EMF from the inductive coils.
>
> Therefore, the motor **cannot be driven directly** from the PIC I/O pin.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the **interfacing circuit diagram** of a unipolar stepper motor with PIC16F877A using ULN2003 driver. List the 4-step full-step sequence (2-phase ON) for clockwise rotation with the corresponding hex values.

**(b)** A 1.8° stepper motor is to be rotated at **60 RPM** in the anticlockwise direction using a PIC16F877A. Calculate the **delay between steps** and the **number of steps** required to rotate the shaft by **180°**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Interfacing Circuit (7 marks)**

The circuit consists of the PIC16F877A's **PORTB (RB0–RB3)** connected to the **IN1–IN4** inputs of the ULN2003A. The ULN2003A's **OUT1–OUT4** are connected to the four coils (A, B, C, D) of the unipolar stepper motor. The common point of the motor coils is tied to **+12 V**, while the ULN2003A sinks current to ground for each activated coil. A freewheeling diode is internally provided in the ULN2003A for each output.

**2-Phase ON Full-Step Sequence (Clockwise):**

| Step | A | B | C | D | Hex Value |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Steps per revolution:

$$
N = \frac{360^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

Step rate at 60 RPM:

$$
f_s = \frac{\text{RPM} \times N}{60} = \frac{60 \times 200}{60} = 200 \text{ steps/sec}
$$

Delay between steps:

$$
T_d = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

Number of steps for 180° rotation:

$$
k = \frac{180^\circ}{\theta_s} = \frac{180^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 100 \text{ steps}
$$

**Anticlockwise direction** is obtained by reversing the sequence order (run the table from step 4 → step 1).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **half-step excitation** mode of a stepper motor. How does it improve resolution compared to full-step mode? Write the 8-step half-step sequence table.

**(b)** A **7.5° stepper motor** is driven by PIC16F877A in **half-step mode** with a software delay of **8 ms** per step. Calculate: (i) the **rotational speed in RPM**, (ii) the **time for 2.5 revolutions**, and (iii) the **resolution improvement factor** over full-step mode.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Half-Step Excitation Mode (7 marks)**

In **half-step mode**, the excitation alternates between **1-phase ON** and **2-phase ON** patterns, producing **8 discrete states** per electrical cycle instead of 4. This causes the rotor to advance by **half the normal step angle** at each pulse, effectively **doubling the resolution**.

**Resolution improvement:**

$$
\theta_{s,\text{half}} = \frac{\theta_{s,\text{full}}}{2}
$$

For a 7.5° motor, half-step angle = 3.75°, giving **96 steps/rev** instead of 48.

**8-Step Half-Step Sequence:**

| Step | A | B | C | D | Hex |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 |
| 8 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Rotational speed in RPM:**

Full-step angle = 7.5°, half-step angle = 3.75°, effective steps per revolution:

$$
N_{\text{half}} = 2 \times \frac{360^\circ}{7.5^\circ} = 2 \times 48 = 96 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

Step rate:

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_d} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 125 \text{ steps/sec}
$$

Speed:

$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{f_s \times 60}{N_{\text{half}}} = \frac{125 \times 60}{96} = \frac{7500}{96} \approx 78.125 \text{ RPM}
$$

**(ii) Time for 2.5 revolutions:**

Total steps:

$$
k_{\text{total}} = 2.5 \times 96 = 240 \text{ steps}
$$

Total time:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 240 \times 8 \text{ ms} = 1920 \text{ ms} = 1.92 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Resolution improvement factor:**

$$
\text{Factor} = \frac{N_{\text{half}}}{N_{\text{full}}} = \frac{96}{48} = 2
$$

The resolution is improved by a factor of **2 (i.e., 100% improvement)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing 1-phase and 2-phase sequences:** Many students write only the wave-drive (1-phase) table and lose 2–3 marks when the question explicitly asks for 2-phase ON.
> 2. **Forgetting the ULN2003A justification:** Examiners award marks specifically for explaining *why* the driver is needed (current amplification, freewheeling diode, isolation). A bare circuit diagram without reasoning loses marks.
> 3. **Unit errors in delay calculation:** Always express $T_d$ in **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (µs)** matching the compiler's `__delay_ms()` or `__delay_us()` macro. Writing 0.005 s instead of 5 ms is marked down.
> 4. **Direction reversal logic:** Reversing the sequence table alone is sufficient — students sometimes incorrectly change the hardware wiring.
> 5. **Half-step table errors:** Skipping alternate 1-phase and 2-phase rows results in a 4-row table — an instant 3-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interfacing Stepper Motor with PIC16F**

- A **stepper motor** is a brushless synchronous motor that moves in **discrete angular steps** synchronized to input pulses.
- **Step angle:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$; **Steps per revolution:** $N = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\theta_s}$.
- Standard motors: **1.8° (200 steps/rev)** and **7.5° (48 steps/rev)**.
- **ULN2003A** is the standard **Darlington driver** between PIC16F and unipolar stepper motor; **L293D/L298** is used for bipolar motors.
- **PIC16F I/O pins can only source ~25 mA**; ULN2003A provides up to **500 mA per channel** with internal freewheeling diodes.
- **Three excitation modes:** Wave drive (1-phase ON, low power), Full-torque drive (2-phase ON, high torque), and **Half-step** (alternating, double resolution).
- **Speed control:** Vary the **software delay** $T_d = \dfrac{60}{\text{RPM} \times N}$ between consecutive steps.
- **Direction control:** Reverse the order of the step sequence in software (CW ↔ CCW).
- **Angle control:** Apply a fixed count of $k = \dfrac{\theta_{\text{desired}}}{\theta_s}$ step pulses.
- For PIC16F at **20 MHz**, instruction cycle = **0.2 µs** ($T_{\text{inst}} = 4 / f_{\text{osc}}$).
- **Half-stepping** doubles effective resolution (96 steps for a 48-step motor) by interleaving 1-phase and 2-phase patterns.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Always state the **sequence table with hex values** when describing drive modes — a verbal description alone is insufficient for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Array Sorting and Manipulations Using Microcontroller PIC16F
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML407 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Array Sorting and Manipulations Using Microcontroller PIC16F

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Array Sorting** is the algorithmic process of rearranging the elements of an array stored in the data memory (RAM) of a microcontroller (such as the **PIC16F877A** / **PIC16F887**) into a specific logical order — typically ascending or descending — by repeatedly comparing adjacent elements and performing conditional swap operations using the CPU's instruction set.

In the context of the **PIC16F series** microcontrollers (Harvard architecture, RISC-based, 14-bit instruction word), array sorting is executed through embedded assembly language or Embedded C programs. The array elements are typically stored contiguously in the **General Purpose Registers (GPR)** area, starting from data memory address `0x20` in **Bank 0**, and manipulated using indexed addressing via the **FSR (File Select Register)** for indirect addressing operations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Sorting a Hand of Playing Cards":**
> Imagine you are holding a hand of 5 playing cards with values [7, 2, 9, 1, 5] and you want them arranged in ascending order. You start from the left, compare card 1 with card 2. If the left one is bigger, you **swap** them. You continue this comparison-and-swap process like a bubble rising to the top — the **largest value "bubbles"** to the rightmost position. After one full pass, the largest card (9) is in its final place. You then repeat for the remaining cards.

This is exactly how the **Bubble Sort algorithm** works inside a microcontroller: the CPU fetches two adjacent array values, compares them using instructions like `SUBWF` or `CPFSLT`, and conditionally swaps them using a temporary register before iterating through the loop.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning in PIC16F Context |
|---|---|
| **Array** | A contiguous block of data memory locations in GPR (0x20–0x7F) |
| **Element** | A single byte (8-bit) value stored at a memory location |
| **Sorting** | Reordering elements using comparison and swap primitives |
| **Swap** | Exchange of values between two memory locations using a temp register |
| **Iteration / Pass** | One complete traversal of the array |
| **Inner Loop** | Compares adjacent pairs and performs swaps |
| **Outer Loop** | Repeats passes until array is fully sorted |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bubble Sort — Operational Logic (Why & How)

The **Bubble Sort** algorithm is the most commonly implemented sorting routine on the PIC16F platform in KTU lab evaluations because it is instruction-efficient, uses only a small register footprint, and clearly demonstrates **nested looping, indexed addressing, and conditional branching** — all core KTU 2024 CO indicators.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**

1. **Why compare adjacent elements?** — Because in a single pass, the largest unsorted element can only move one step to the right. We must compare neighbors to give it a "push."
2. **Why use a temporary register?** — PIC16F has **no direct memory-to-memory swap instruction**. We must load one value into `W`, then store it in `TEMP`, before overwriting the original location.
3. **Why nested loops?** — The outer loop tracks how many passes are done; the inner loop performs the actual comparisons. After each pass, the largest element "bubbles up" to the end, so we can **shrink** the inner loop by one iteration each pass (optimization).
4. **Why use FSR for indirect addressing?** — Direct addressing in PIC16F requires the full 7-bit address in the instruction (only 7 bits available), which limits range. **Indirect addressing** via `FSR` lets us walk through a contiguous array elegantly.

### 2.2 The Bubble Sort Logic — Step by Step

```
For i = 0 to N-2:
    For j = 0 to (N-2-i):
        IF A[j] > A[j+1]:
            SWAP(A[j], A[j+1])
```

The **swap primitive** in PIC assembly requires **3 register operations**:
- `MOVF A[j], W`  →  load first element into W
- `MOVWF TEMP`     →  store it in temporary register
- `MOVF A[j+1], W` →  load second element into W
- `MOVWF A[j]`     →  overwrite first with second
- `MOVF TEMP, W`   →  reload first from temp
- `MOVWF A[j+1]`   →  overwrite second with first

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical, algorithmic, and architectural parameters** essential for KTU 2024 lab viva and ESE on PIC16F array sorting.

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Comparisons (Worst Case)** | $C_{max} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | For an array of size $N$ |
| **Maximum Swaps (Worst Case)** | $S_{max} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | Reverse-sorted input |
| **Time Complexity (Average/Worst)** | $O(N^2)$ | Quadratic growth |
| **Time Complexity (Best)** | $O(N)$ | Already sorted (with flag optimization) |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ | Only one `TEMP` register needed |
| **Number of Outer Loop Iterations** | $N - 1$ | For $N$ elements |
| **Number of Inner Loop Iterations (Pass i)** | $N - 1 - i$ | Decreases per pass |
| **PIC16F GPR Array Base Address** | `0x20` | Start of Bank 0 user RAM |
| **PIC16F GPR Array End Address** | `0x7F` (PIC16F877A) | 96 bytes available |
| **FSR Indirect Addressing Range** | `0x00` – `0xFF` | Full data memory access |
| **Instruction Word Width** | **14 bits** | All instructions are single-cycle (4 clock cycles) |
| **Clock per Instruction (CPI)** | $T_{inst} = 4 \times T_{osc}$ | At **20 MHz** oscillator: $T_{inst} = 200$ ns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on KTU Valuation:** Examiners expect students to state the **time complexity $O(N^2)$** and the **space complexity $O(1)$** explicitly. Marks are awarded for **counting the swap and compare operations**, not just stating the algorithm.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Array sorting in microcontrollers is foundational for:

- **Sensor Data Processing** — Sorting ADC readings to find median (noise filtering).
- **Display Routines** — Ranking values on a 7-segment or LCD.
- **Communication Protocols** — Sorting received bytes by priority (e.g., CAN bus arbitration).
- **Control Systems** — Ranking PID error values for adaptive gain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Trace — Bubble Sort on Array of N = 4

Let us **derive step-by-step** what happens when the following unsorted array is stored in PIC16F GPR starting at address `0x20`:

$$
A = [\,8,\ 3,\ 5,\ 1\,]
$$

We aim to sort this in **ascending order**. Let $A[i]$ denote the value at memory address `0x20 + i`.

**Pass 1 (i = 0): Inner loop runs for j = 0, 1, 2 (3 comparisons)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1.1: } j=0, \text{ compare } A[0] \text{ with } A[1] \\
&\quad 8 > 3 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{SWAP} \\
&\quad \text{Resulting array: } A = [\,3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 1\,] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{Step 1.2: } j=1, \text{ compare } A[1] \text{ with } A[2] \\
&\quad 8 > 5 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{SWAP} \\
&\quad \text{Resulting array: } A = [\,3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1\,] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{Step 1.3: } j=2, \text{ compare } A[2] \text{ with } A[3] \\
&\quad 8 > 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{SWAP} \\
&\quad \text{Resulting array: } A = [\,3,\ 5,\ 1,\ \mathbf{8}\,] \\
&\quad \text{(Element 8 is now in its FINAL position)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 2 (i = 1): Inner loop runs for j = 0, 1 (2 comparisons)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 2.1: } j=0, \text{ compare } A[0] \text{ with } A[1] \\
&\quad 3 < 5 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{NO SWAP} \\
&\quad \text{Array unchanged: } A = [\,3,\ 5,\ 1,\ 8\,] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{Step 2.2: } j=1, \text{ compare } A[1] \text{ with } A[2] \\
&\quad 5 > 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{SWAP} \\
&\quad \text{Resulting array: } A = [\,3,\ 1,\ \mathbf{5},\ \mathbf{8}\,] \\
&\quad \text{(Element 5 is now in its final position)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 3 (i = 2): Inner loop runs for j = 0 (1 comparison)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 3.1: } j=0, \text{ compare } A[0] \text{ with } A[1] \\
&\quad 3 > 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{SWAP} \\
&\quad \text{Resulting array: } A = [\,\mathbf{1},\ \mathbf{3},\ \mathbf{5},\ \mathbf{8}\,] \\
&\quad \text{ARRAY IS NOW SORTED IN ASCENDING ORDER.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Total Operations for N = 4

| Pass $i$ | Comparisons | Swaps in this Trace | Largest Bubble Reached |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 3 | 8 (final) |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 5 (final) |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 3 (final) |
| **Total** | **6** | **5** | — |

**Verification using the KTU formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6 \;\; \text{comparisons (matches)} \\[6pt]
S_{max} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = 6 \;\; \text{maximum possible swaps} \\
S_{actual} &= 5 \;\; \text{(fewer because pass 2 had one no-swap)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Execution Time on PIC16F

Suppose the **oscillator frequency** $f_{osc} = 20 \text{ MHz}$ and the array size is $N = 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{osc} &= \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^6} = 50 \text{ ns} \\[6pt]
T_{instruction} &= 4 \times T_{osc} = 4 \times 50 \text{ ns} = 200 \text{ ns} \\[6pt]
C_{max} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = \frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45 \text{ comparisons} \\[6pt]
\text{Instructions per comparison (load, compare, branch)} &\approx 8 \\[6pt]
T_{sort} &\approx 45 \times 8 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 72{,}000 \text{ ns} = 72 \;\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the **PIC16F executes a 10-element bubble sort in under 100 microseconds** at 20 MHz — a key takeaway for KTU viva questions on real-time feasibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bubble Sort Flowchart (PIC16F Implementation Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block is the **Algorithm-Level Flow** showing how the PIC16F CPU executes the bubble sort. The `node1`–`node11` are alphanumeric identifiers as per KTU diagram safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1([START: Load N into COUNT1]) --> node2[Initialize FSR to 0x20 - array base]
    node2 --> node3[OUTER LOOP: i = 0]
    node3 --> node4{Is i less than N minus 1?}
    node4 -- NO --> node11([END: Sorted array in RAM])
    node4 -- YES --> node5[INNER LOOP: j = 0]
    node5 --> node6{Is j less than N minus 1 minus i?}
    node6 -- NO --> node10[Increment i, GoTo OUTER LOOP]
    node6 -- YES --> node7[Load A j and A j plus 1 via FSR]
    node7 --> node8{Is A j greater than A j plus 1?}
    node8 -- NO --> node9[Increment j, GoTo INNER LOOP]
    node8 -- YES --> nodeA[Perform SWAP using TEMP register]
    nodeA --> node9
    node9 --> node6
    node10 --> node4
```

### 4.2 PIC16F877A Data Memory Map (Bank 0 — Array Storage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[0x00 to 0x0B: Special Function Registers SFR] --> node2[0x0C to 0x1F: Additional SFRs]
    node2 --> node3[0x20: ARRAY BASE - Element 0]
    node3 --> node4[0x21: Element 1]
    node4 --> node5[0x22: Element 2]
    node5 --> node6[0x23: Element 3]
    node6 --> node7[0x24: Element 4]
    node7 --> node8[0x25: TEMP register for SWAP]
    node8 --> node9[0x26 to 0x7F: Free GPR space]
    node9 --> node10[0x7F: End of Bank 0 GPR]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Program Memory - Flash - 14-bit Instructions] --> B[Instruction Decoder and Control Unit]
    B --> C[ALU - Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    C --> D[Working Register W]
    D --> E[File Select Register FSR]
    E --> F[Data Memory RAM - GPR Array]
    F --> G[Temporary Register TEMP]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Result back to RAM]
```

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU lab record, students are expected to draw the **MPLAB X IDE simulation output** showing register windows with the array values updating after each pass. Always **highlight the final sorted array in the watch window**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** 
**Define array sorting in the context of PIC16F microcontrollers. Name any two sorting algorithms that can be implemented on it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Array sorting on a PIC16F microcontroller is the process of rearranging data elements stored in the General Purpose Registers (GPR) of the data memory (RAM) in a specific order (ascending or descending) by executing comparison and conditional swap operations using the CPU instructions.

Two sorting algorithms implementable on PIC16F:
1. **Bubble Sort** — Compare-and-swap adjacent elements repeatedly.
2. **Selection Sort** — Find the minimum element and place it in the correct position iteratively.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for naming two algorithms with brief description)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** 
**State the time and space complexity of the bubble sort algorithm used in PIC16F array sorting. Why is space complexity constant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Time Complexity:** $O(N^2)$ in average and worst case; $O(N)$ in best case (with flag-based early termination).
- **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — only a single **TEMP register** is required for swap operations; no auxiliary data structure is created.
- **Reason for constant space:** The PIC16F performs in-place sorting by overwriting existing memory locations; the array itself is reused to hold the sorted result, and only one byte of temporary storage is needed regardless of array size $N$.

*(1 mark each for time complexity, space complexity, and justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often **forget to declare the array size** in their program — instant **deduction of 1 mark**.
> - Marks are awarded for the **swap logic** (using TEMP register) — writing `MOVF` and `MOVWF` correctly is critical.
> - Always state the **worst-case comparison count** using the $N(N-1)/2$ formula.
> - In simulation-based questions, failing to show the **MPLAB X watch window output** loses up to **2 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Write an PIC16F assembly language program to sort an array of **5 unsigned bytes** stored in data memory starting at address `0x20`, in **ascending order** using the **Bubble Sort algorithm**. Clearly explain the role of the **FSR register** and the **TEMP register** in your program. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the input array is `[45, 12, 78, 23, 56]` (in decimal), **trace the algorithm step-by-step** showing the array contents after each pass. Calculate the total number of comparisons performed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Program (7 marks)**

**Register Allocation:**

| Register | Address | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `FSR` | `0x04` | Indirect address pointer for array walk |
| `TEMP` | `0x25` | Temporary storage for swap |
| `COUNT1` | `0x26` | Outer loop counter (Pass counter) |
| `COUNT2` | `0x27` | Inner loop counter (Comparison counter) |
| `N` | `0x28` | Array size (set to 5) |

**Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        BCF     STATUS, RP0      ; Select Bank 0
        BSF     STATUS, RP1      ; (For PIC16F877A Bank selection as needed)

        MOVLW   0x05
        MOVWF   N                ; N = 5 (array size)

        MOVLW   D'5'
        MOVWF   COUNT1           ; Outer loop counter = 5

OUTER   MOVF    N, W
        SUBWF   COUNT1, W        ; W = COUNT1 - N
        BTFSC   STATUS, Z        ; If COUNT1 == N, exit
        GOTO    DONE

        MOVF    N, W
        SUBWF   COUNT1, W
        MOVWF   COUNT2           ; Inner limit = N - COUNT1

        MOVLW   0x20
        MOVWF   FSR              ; FSR points to A[0]

INNER   MOVF    COUNT2, W
        SUBLW   0x00
        BTFSC   STATUS, Z        ; If COUNT2 == 0, end inner
        GOTO    ENDINNER

        MOVF    INDF, W          ; W = A[j]
        MOVWF   TEMP             ; TEMP = A[j]
        INCF    FSR, F           ; FSR points to A[j+1]
        MOVF    INDF, W          ; W = A[j+1]

        SUBWF   TEMP, W          ; W = A[j] - A[j+1]
        BTFSC   STATUS, C        ; If A[j] > A[j+1] (carry clear), swap
        GOTO    NOSWAP

        ; SWAP routine
        MOVF    INDF, W          ; W = A[j+1]
        DECF    FSR, F
        MOVWF   INDF             ; A[j] = A[j+1]
        INCF    FSR, F
        MOVF    TEMP, W
        MOVWF   INDF             ; A[j+1] = old A[j]
        DECF    FSR, F           ; FSR back to A[j]

NOSWAP  INCF    FSR, F           ; Move to next pair
        DECF    COUNT2, F
        GOTO    INNER

ENDINNER DECF    COUNT1, F
        GOTO    OUTER

DONE    GOTO    DONE             ; Infinite loop (halt)
```

**Role of FSR:** Acts as an **indirect address pointer**. The `INDF` register (at `0x00`) accesses whatever address FSR currently points to. Incrementing FSR walks through the array — replacing multi-instruction direct addressing with a single `INDF` access. **(2 marks)**

**Role of TEMP:** PIC16F lacks a memory-to-memory swap instruction. `TEMP` holds one value while the other is moved, enabling the **3-step swap** (load, store, restore). **(2 marks)**

**Marks Distribution:** Program logic (3) + FSR explanation (2) + TEMP explanation (2) = **7 marks**

---

**(b) Trace and Comparison Count (7 marks)**

Initial array: `[45, 12, 78, 23, 56]`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Pass 1: } [45, 12, 78, 23, 56] \rightarrow [12, 45, 23, 56, 78] \\
&\quad \text{Comparisons: 4} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Pass 2: } [12, 45, 23, 56, 78] \rightarrow [12, 23, 45, 56, 78] \\
&\quad \text{Comparisons: 3} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Pass 3: } [12, 23, 45, 56, 78] \rightarrow \text{No swap} \\
&\quad \text{Comparisons: 2} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Pass 4: } [12, 23, 45, 56, 78] \rightarrow \text{No swap} \\
&\quad \text{Comparisons: 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total comparisons:**

$$
C_{total} = 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10 = \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Final sorted array:** `[12, 23, 45, 56, 78]` **(3 marks for trace, 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for total)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **data memory organization** of PIC16F877A. Indicate the address range of the **GPR** where user arrays are typically stored. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **direct addressing** and **indirect addressing** modes in PIC16F. Show how indirect addressing using **FSR** is used to access the third element of an array stored at `0x20`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Data Memory Organization (7 marks)**

The PIC16F877A has **368 bytes of RAM** divided into:
- **Special Function Registers (SFRs):** `0x00` to `0x1F` (addresses common to all banks)
- **General Purpose Registers (GPRs):** `0x20` to `0x7F` (Bank 0) + `0xA0` to `0xEF` (Bank 1) = **192 bytes** of user-accessible RAM
- **Bank selection** is controlled by **RP0 and RP1 bits** of the `STATUS` register.

**GPR Address Range for User Arrays:** **`0x20` to `0x7F`** (96 bytes in Bank 0)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeB0[Bank 0 - 0x00 to 0x7F]
    nodeSFR[SFR Area - 0x00 to 0x1F]
    nodeGPR[GPR Area - 0x20 to 0x7F - 96 bytes]
    nodeB0 --> nodeSFR
    nodeB0 --> nodeGPR
```

**Marks:** Memory map diagram (3) + GPR range (2) + Bank concept (2) = **7 marks**

---

**(b) Addressing Modes (7 marks)**

| Feature | Direct Addressing | Indirect Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| **Address specified in** | Instruction itself (7 bits) | Contents of `FSR` register |
| **Register used** | Operand field of instruction | `INDF` register (`0x00`) |
| **Use case** | Single fixed memory location | Arrays, loops, pointer traversal |
| **Instruction example** | `MOVF 0x20, W` | `MOVF INDF, W` (reads `[FSR]`) |
| **Range** | 0x00 – 0x7F only (7 bits) | Full 0x00 – 0xFF via FSR |

**Accessing the third element of an array at `0x20`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Third element} = A[2] \;\;\text{located at}\;\; 0x20 + 2 = 0x22 \\[6pt]
&\textbf{Assembly sequence:} \\[4pt]
&\text{MOVLW   0x22} \quad \text{; Load address 0x22 into W} \\
&\text{MOVWF   FSR}  \quad \text{; FSR now points to A[2]} \\
&\text{MOVF    INDF, W} \quad \text{; W = value at A[2]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks:** Direct vs Indirect table (3) + Accessing A[2] sequence (3) + Brief explanation (1) = **7 marks**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE & Lab Viva:**

- **Array base address in PIC16F877A** is **`0x20`** (start of GPR in Bank 0).
- **Bubble Sort complexity:** $O(N^2)$ time, $O(1)$ space — **must be stated explicitly** in viva.
- **Maximum comparisons formula:** $C_{max} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$.
- **No direct memory-to-memory swap exists** in PIC16F — always use a `TEMP` register with `MOVF` and `MOVWF`.
- **FSR + INDF** = indirect addressing — primary method to walk through arrays in PIC16F assembly.
- **One instruction cycle** = **4 oscillator cycles** → at **20 MHz**, $T_{inst} = 200$ ns.
- **Instruction word width = 14 bits**; all instructions are **single-cycle** except branches (2 cycles).
- **For descending order sort:** invert the comparison condition (use `BTFSS STATUS, C` instead of `BTFSC`).
- **Common lab mistakes to avoid:** 
  - Forgetting to clear `RP0`/`RP1` bits of `STATUS` register → wrong bank accessed.
  - Not resetting FSR before each new pass of the outer loop.
  - Off-by-one errors in inner loop bound (use `N-1-i`, not `N-i`).
- **Optimization flag trick (for viva bonus):** Add a `SWAPPED` flag register — set to 1 on any swap, checked at end of each pass. If no swap occurred, array is sorted — early exit reduces best case to $O(N)$.
- **For Embedded C (XC8) version:** Use `unsigned char arr[5]` stored in a `bank1` qualifier, sort using two nested `for` loops with a `temp` variable.
